body:
Hi Ioc,
I was able to find a solution to my issue as described [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/69614700/import-css-file-into-es6-returns-string-instead-of-object).

On the accepted answer you'll find my analysis to the whole issue and 2 solution concepts.

Although I would have preferred the inline approach (1), I didn't want to use an undocumented and hard-to-read syntax which is likely to change in the future.

Thus, I resembled to setting up a customized webpack config (2).
Please note, that I'm not setting it up from scratch. Instead I provide a function that 

- searches for the correct ruleset
- excludes a certain filename pattern
- adds a custom rule for that filename pattern

This solution works like a charm. The downside of this approach is that I need to stick to a naming convention.

In my angular apps I used [@angular-builders/custom-webpack](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@angular-builders/custom-webpack) to set up my webpack configurator.

Please let me know if you need further instructions.

author: allaire

body:
I think we probably have you covered, but it's still in an early state.

> * Add/inject additional `CssModule`s from our plugin

In webpack you usually don't "generate" modules, but instead write or generate imports that lead to the creating of a module.

> * Generate JS modules similar to `CSS modules` but with runtime functionality backed in, and having different export structure

This sounds like you can create a JS module that wraps a css module.

> * Exclude `.st.css` file extension from the built-in webpack CSS process or somehow use the built-in integrations as a post-process for asset handling and chunking 

Webpack allows to configure the css support via rules.

> * Optimize the JS modules and emitted CSS respectively before emitting bundles. (e.g. make classnames shorter and remove unused components CSS)

The current experimental css modules implementation in webpack does mangle class names. Removing unused rules is planned but not yet implemented.

body:
Admittedly it's been a while since I first started thinking about this problem, so I'll have to remember specifics slowly.

The most recent example that made me think of this was that `contentBase` was changed to `static`, with new/different syntax, for I think the devServer config.

I know the many ways of configuring loaders (`use`, `loader`, `loaders`) has changed a lot in confusing ways over time, especially what shapes of values are valid for those properties, as there are a lot of "shorthand" ways of configuring those as well, so there's many shapes for just that part for each version of the config schema.
author: phoenixeliot-practicum

body:
An example point of confusion:
- [v4 docs](https://v4.webpack.js.org/configuration/dev-server/) lists the property as `devServer.contentBase`
- [v5 docs](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/dev-server/) say `devServer.static`
- but [the migration guide for migrating to v4](https://github.com/webpack/webpack-dev-server/blob/master/migration-v4.md) (of webpack-dev-server, I notice now) implies that `contentBase` changed to `static` between v3-v4, not from v4-v5.

Maybe this is because the version numbers for webpack and webpack-dev-server don't match? But it took me writing out this whole message to have that thought, and I have no idea if it's right. Either way, it's confusing.
author: phoenixeliot-practicum

body:
Plugins that are bundled with Webpack changing how you're meant to use them between minor versions of webpack is also a big source of confusion, since they affect how you have to write your webpack config as well.

I'm not sure exactly how the bundling works with pinning versions, or if they exist independently instead of being pinned together. I haven't checked on that in a while.
author: phoenixeliot-practicum

body:
webpack-dev-server is not bundled with webpack. You have it separately in your package.json.

We are very careful to not introduce any breaking change in webpack during minor version, and afaik we never introduced a breaking change to the schema. What we did in the past was to make the schema a bit stricter to disallow some values that were allowed before but not intended and broken.

I'm pretty sure the same is true for webpack-dev-server, but independently from webpack as it's a separate dependency.

body:
> Digging through the chunk graph, I can see that there are several paths from the chunk(s) containing the requesting modules up the parentage in the chunk graph where the requested module is not included. This seems like a core webpack bug, but it must clearly be an edge case given the lack of other complaints. Any idea of what validation is supposed to exist for this scenario that may be failing?

Can you explain how you found this this bug? I will allow to say what is wrong
author: alexander-akait

body:
I’ll fetch the code and output when I log back into work, but from the browser error console I’ve found two separate pairs of requestor-requested modules that repo this. I verified the issue in a debug session with these modules: 
1. Find the chunk(s) containing the requestor module
2. For each chunk group the chunk(s) are in, see if the requested module is in the same group.
3. If not, for *each* parent chunk group, check to see if the requested module is in the group. If not, repeat for *each* parent chunk group, until reaching an entry chunk.

Doing this for one pair, I found maybe a dozen paths from an entry chunk to the chunk of the requestor module, and only one path included a chunk with the requested module.
author: MLoughry

body: Does every entry has its own [runtime](https://webpack.js.org/concepts/entry-points/#entrydescription-object) ?
author: vankop

body: Yes
author: MLoughry

body: Or rather, we don't do anything to split out the runtime. We use the HTML webpack plugin to create HTML files for each entry, and the entries are defined as a simple mapping of name to module with no other settings.
author: MLoughry

body: Can someone point out where the chunking is performed in the compilation? I can try to investigate from there.
author: MLoughry

body:
It's in `lib/buildChunkGraph.js`. But beware that's not easy to follow, since it's very performance sensitive...

I think there must be something special in your application that causes that...
* Do you have some custom splitChunks config?
* Any plugin that does something with the chunk graph?
* Anything that injects scripts into the HTML?
* Since every entry has it's own runtime: You must not load multiple entries per page. Only a single runtime is allow per page.
* Is `shouldGoAboveDividerInCommandBarOverflowMenu` a side-effect-free module? (I've see your app before, and that was very common)
  * when yes, is `owa-mail-commandbar-editor/lib/index.js` using `shouldGoAboveDividerInCommandBarOverflowMenu` only conditionally? That means some exports use the imported bindings. So whether `shouldGoAboveDividerInCommandBarOverflowMenu` is needed or not depends on which exports are used from `owa-mail-commandbar-editor/lib/index.js`.

> However, there are several paths from the entry chunks (all lowercase names) to the requesting module's chunk 20769, but only some go through "MailBoot", and none of them go through 79590.

That's seems likely to be problem, but I'm not 100% sure. Maybe the module is not used in that path.

I could imagine multiple places where this could be caused by:

* chunk graph building (but I actually doubt it's a problem here, that would be visible in more applications)
* split chunks
* runtime-specific side-effect-free modules elimination (that's rarely used and would be my first guess)

Let's try to isolate the problem:

> runtime-specific side-effect-free modules elimination

Could you try to disable that with `optimization.usedExports: "global"` and/or `optimization.sideEffects: false`?

> split chunks

Could you try to disable that with `optimization.splitChunks: false`?
author: sokra

body:
`optimization.usedExports: "global"` seems to have resolved the problem, and reduced bundle sizes in most bundles we track, so this is probably what we'll use. I may investigate the other suggestions as I have time.

> Do you have some custom splitChunks config?

We set `optimization.splitChunks.maxAsyncRequests` to 30, but otherwise no.

> Any plugin that does something with the chunk graph?

Not to my knowledge. Certainly no plugin of ours, nor do I think any of the public plugins we use do anything like that.

> Anything that injects scripts into the HTML?

Just `html-webpack-plugin`

> Since every entry has it's own runtime: You must not load multiple entries per page. Only a single runtime is allow per page.

Yup, we have separate pages with `html-webpack-plugin`

> Is shouldGoAboveDividerInCommandBarOverflowMenu a side-effect-free module? (I've see your app before, and that was very common)

Yes, it only exports a pure function (and the only import is a Typescript type import that is removed by `tsc`)

> when yes, is owa-mail-commandbar-editor/lib/index.js using shouldGoAboveDividerInCommandBarOverflowMenu only conditionally? That means some exports use the imported bindings. So whether shouldGoAboveDividerInCommandBarOverflowMenu is needed or not depends on which exports are used from owa-mail-commandbar-editor/lib/index.js.

It is exported from `owa-mail-commandbar-editor/lib/index.js`
author: MLoughry

body:
The default/implicit target is `web`.  For that target, the output can be ESM.  This can be seen after removing `externals` from the above configuration and running `yarn webpack`.  That said, the default target `web` does support ESM.

When `externals` is not removed, webpack says, that the target=`web` does not support EcmaScriptModule syntax.  This statement is not correct, as the previous paragraph explains, why the `web` target supports EcmaScriptModule syntax.
author: dilyanpalauzov

author: vankop

body: Why shall I pass any target?  When the webpack-output is ESM, and it is in this example ESM, then webpack shall assume that the target supports ESM syntax.  Currently when the output is ESM, webpack is unsure, whether the target supports ESM syntax. There is no place for such doubts.
author: dilyanpalauzov

body: Because not every env supports ESM, maybe we revisit it late (in the next major release), but currently better output the error, web can be in ES3, ES5, and other
author: alexander-akait

body: When webpack is told to output in ESM syntax, please tweak webpack to assume, that the target support ESM syntax. (Thinking that the target does not support ESM syntax, while emitting ESM output, makes no sense).

body: You can use ModuleFederation with other target, you can use `DllPlugin` with other type, you can apply NodeJs plugins and not only, which can't support ESM in some cases, you set different chunkLoading for entries https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/types.d.ts#L3193, which can't support ESM, do you need more?

body: The thing is that the injected code _doesn't_ produce invalid syntax in the compiled code. It's being used as `__non_webpack_import__('./foo.js')` should be replaced to `import('./foo.js')` -- that's valid. The problem is that the way the DefinePlugin is parsing expressions causes it to throw an error.

body: In this case you need to create webpack plugin and register `__non_webpack_import__` as webpack API and then do code generation with `import`, DefinePlugin was not design for this, like we do for `__non_webpack_require__`  https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/04039ca99c9a5575ecb041796c6a0858d7be591d/lib/APIPlugin.js#L54
author: alexander-akait

author: vankop

body:
I mean, it shouldn't be a parser error. As a consumer of webpack, I define that `__non_webpack_import__` is equivalent to import, and then expect to be able to use it like I would `import`. None of _my own_ code is invalid or would result in a parse error. It's an implementation detail of Webpack that -- as I've discovered today -- `DefinePlugin` can only be used with valid JS expressions.

I suggest either (a) removing this restriction, or (b) dealing with parse errors and presenting them to the user in a better way. For example in the latter case, Webpack could re-throw an error with a message like `ParseError: expression "import" from the DefinePlugin is not a valid expression`. That would document this behavior and allow consumers to figure out what's going wrong without having to debug webpack internals like I did.

Though, as mentioned, the code emitted from the compiler after the replacement happens would be valid, it's a limitation of the way DefinePlugin is implemented that the plugin couldn't make the replacement.

body:
@Musadiqkhan-nullbrainer we had this same exact issue (not just the error, but also downgrading to 5.24.0 versus 5.24.1 would fix things). in our case we had the `ModuleConcatenationPlugin` defined twice (when mode is production and in the plugins block) which caused the issue. More info https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/17100 

you may have some plugin causing an issue in your production config, try disabling them one by one and see if the error persists.
author: bmarti44

body:
Had the same issue after a recent upgrade to Webpack 5. To reiterate @bmarti44's point (or, at least, what I think might be the issue):

If you have the ModuleConcantenationPlugin included in your webpack config's plugin section like this: 

`new webpack.optimize.ModuleConcatenationPlugin()`

and that same config has mode set to `production`, then I believe this issue might occur, since according to the documentation: "By default this plugin is already enabled in [production mode](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/mode/#mode-production) and disabled otherwise." It does seem a little odd that this would be the case, but removing the plugin instantiation fixed things. 

Would it be possible to add this detail to the documentation (either [here](https://webpack.js.org/plugins/module-concatenation-plugin/) or in the Webpack 5 upgrade docs)? I'd be happy to do so if it's easier

I fixed it by adding transloco to the shared mapping in the webpack.config file:

```
plugins: [
    new ModuleFederationPlugin({
        library: { type: "module" },

        shared: share({
          "@angular/core": { singleton: true, strictVersion: true, requiredVersion: 'auto' },
          "@angular/common": { singleton: true, strictVersion: true, requiredVersion: 'auto' },
          "@angular/common/http": { singleton: true, strictVersion: true, requiredVersion: 'auto' },
          "@angular/router": { singleton: true, strictVersion: true, requiredVersion: 'auto' },
          "@ngneat/transloco": { singleton: true, strictVersion: true, requiredVersion: 'auto' },

          ...sharedMappings.getDescriptors()
        })

    }),
    sharedMappings.getPlugin()
  ],
```
...

In best case: Yes the worker should load the same chunk as the main browser thread (as my diagram indicates). This would ensure that:
 
1. The browser will not need to load an additional separate chunk or file but take the one already in the local browser cache. 
2. Any additional customizations (e.g. plugins) packed together into the chunk of my library will also be available within the worker.

> Do you looking this https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/12638?

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/12638 sounds similar from the use case to my first problem I noticed but also the discussion there confuses me a bit. body: Why you need URL to script? It is real bad idea, you should rely `import.meta.url`, name can be renamed/hashed/moved to another dir by bundlers
author: alexander-akait

body:
>Why you need URL to script?

Please read through my post on StackOverflow, I think it really describes well in full extend my concrete use case why I need the URL to the chunk. It is linked above in my initial post: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/70733934/how-to-integrate-own-library-with-webpack-which-needs-to-span-webworkers-and-aud

> you should rely import.meta.url

This does not work. `import.meta.url` is rewritten to a file URL pointing to the previous source of the module (before packing) by WebPack. This URL is pretty useless outside a local Node.js environment.  

> name can be renamed/hashed/moved to another dir by bundlers

This is exactly my use case. Bundlers (WebPack in this case) is taking my library and packing it into a chunk. I need to know the exact URL to this chunk within my library which was packed into this chunk. I don't need any access to my original file which was packed into the chunk.
author: Danielku15

body:
I prepared you quickly a Stackblitz example: https://stackblitz.com/edit/github-hhejac?file=src/mylib.js

The `/dist/no-webpack.html` version as ES Module works fine. We can see in correct order: 
1. Hello Main Browser UI Thread!
2. Starting library
3. Output of all variants where `import.meta.url` points to the **url** providing mylib
4. An error from the `new Worker(import.meta.url)` which can be ignored. 
5. Hello Web Worker!

The `/dist/index.html` shows the WebPack bundled version. As you can see, none of the `getMyLibScriptFile` variants allow me to resolve the `chunk-mylib.js` within the now embedded `mylib.js`. Also looking at the rewritten source of `chunk-mylib.js` in getMyLibScriptFile we can see that no variant works well. 

1. `currentScript` is pointing to main.js
2. `__webpack__public_path__` points to `https://github-hhejac--5000.local.webcontainer.io/dist/`
3. `import.meta.url` was rewritten to the file-url where originally mylib.js was: `file:///home/projects/github-hhejac/src/mylib.js`
4. `new Url(import.meta.url, import.meta.url)` points to an additional JS asset file being generated with a hash. In my real project there is also additional code added to this file which makes it unusable in the web. 
5. `new Worker(import.meta.url)` is also rewritten to the file url. 

I need some way to resolve `/dist/chunk-mylib.js` to spawn my Worker.
author: Danielku15

body:
`new Worker(import.meta.url)` does not work in webpack. you can use only
```
new Worker(new URL('./worker.js'), import.meta.url)
```
author: vankop

body:
`new Worker(import.meta.url)` was "just" one of the many attempts to get one version which gives me the right urls. I would be happy to use something like `new Worker(new URL('./worker.js'), import.meta.url)` but this just brings me to the first problem again: I do not know what to write for `./worker.js` within `mylib` as it will be the chunk name which WebPack defines. 

`mylib` is usually an external library which comes through NPM and is then embedded by developers through WebPack into the final app. Hence I need a construct which I can put into my JavaScript which will result in correct behavior in WebPack. 

To go more towards a solution driven approach instead of discussing what does not work today: 

It would be great if there is something like `__webpack_chunk_name__ `, `import.meta.chunkUrl` or any other construct which will be replaced by WebPack with the absolute URL to `/dist/chunk-mylib.js`. Just the same way it already replaces `import.meta.url` with the file path. 

I see multiple possibilities where some might have unexpected side effects: 

- Introduce an officially available variable like `__webpack_chunk_name__` or `import.meta.chunkUrl` which will give you the URL of the chunk. 
- Introduce a new code construct which is detected and rewritten like `new URL(import.meta.url)` rather pointing to the chunk URL (which must not be a file:// url). In best case the construct would be compatible with all environments (web, node.js,..)
- Introduce a new setting into WebPack which changes the behavior of rewriting `import.meta.url`. It would allow you to configure WebPack to either: point to the file URL of the original source file (current behavior), point to the file URL of the chunk (new behavior 1 for node.js environments), point to the relative output path of the chunk respecting `__webpack_public_path__`.

body:
Unfortunately uch one-sentence answers do not really help me any further. It does not provide me any real step forward how to really solve my case. It is impossible for me, with not being a WebPack expert, to derive what you actually propose to change within the setup of my library, and what I should advise to integrators to reach a working solution. 😞 I am hoping for a bit more constructive feedback which actually applies to my actual usecase and setup.

Workers have always been a huge pain in bundlers and I know that it is not a "simple" topic and might not apply to the majority of users which don't use any Web Worker or Audio Worklet features in their applications. But I still think it is a valid "advanced" topic which should be solvable in WebPack in some way. I as library author only have limited capabilities to suggest what actual integrators using WebPack, Rollup or Parcel can do. 😢 

To challenge your statement that I should change something: For me personally this behavior is a potentially missing feature or bug in WebPack. It should never ever put file urls to a source file into a packed output file. IMO in the context of bundling you would never expect someone to still access the original source files anymore but `import.meta.url` is translated to that. The only use-case I potentially see, might be debug related code like logging where you're interested into the original source, but for this sourcemaps exist. Also the file URLs will not work in any Web Context and considering that WebPack is the main bundler used in Angular, Vue and React enviroments this seems an incorrect behavior. 🤔

I created a example repo here: https://github.com/Danielku15/webpack-worker
Just start the repository with `npm i && npm start` and then open the website. You will find a real world example (with&without webpack) and a simplified setup with a small fake library trying to do the same thing: Spawning a worker using the file it is contained in. 

The samples without WebPack are showing what I want to achieve from a runtime behavior. The ones with WebPack show the challenge. In the `getMyLibScriptFile` of `mylib.js` I need to obtain somehow the single or multiple URLs required to startup correctly my library. 

 >Again you should never rely on chunk filename, it is really bad idea, bundler can apply any count of optimizations and split chunks on two/three/more and more and more.

I don't see a problem in this if WebPack allows me to obtain the correct URLs so that I can include/import them in the Workers. I cannot workaround the standard here. Workers (and Audio Worklets) simply need one file which acts as entry point for the background process. 
Also I don't see it as a problem if I advise the user to follow a specific chunk configuration for my library. I can live with some trade-offs as long the solution works and is simple for my integrators. 

> I see you use new URL(import.meta.url, import.meta.url), that is interesting, what you expected?

It was simply one of the many attempts to get somehow the URL to the chunk. I [read](https://webpack.js.org/guides/web-workers/) about the details that WebPack treats `new Url('./something.js', import.meta.url)` in a special way so I hoped to resolve the own file url with this mechanism.
author: Danielku15
alexander-akait

body:
> Why we need it in webpack, why we need browser API on webpack side? 

I'm not sure if I can follow you here 😅 (might be a bit of a language barrier here) . Let's see if I understand you right: you are wondering why webpack is supposed to offer a certain functionality which is supposed to be available through standard browser APIs? 

In this case my answer would be: With the latest PR merged above, I would have to check if `import.meta.url` is now working correctly and allows me to resolve the "quasi" entry point to my library. But generally I would also say: WebPack rewrites code quite significantly and is introducing an own custom module system. On top, as you indicated: WebPack does quite some complex parts with splitting potentially files apart into multiple chunks. 

Looking at the compile output of "chunk-mylib.js" or "chunk-alphatab.js": There is no standard ESM (import/export) in place anymore, the whole dependency management is done through WebPack. 

That's why I think only WebPack can offer the required features to provide to the rewritten/bundled libraries the URL of the file it is contained in. `import.meta.url` is rewritten today, and might not be rewritten tomorrow. But also with WebPack rewriting ESM to WebPack-require I would be surprised if the default browser API `import.meta.url` would provide me the URL to the file which contains my library. 

In fact, likely these chunks even are not usable by anyone outside the main WebPack entry point. 

While the technical solution is still unclear at this point. I hope the use case what I want to achieve is understandable.I can try to rephrase it another time if this helps in understanding what I want to achieve. ☺️ 

> Why don't use currentScript

Due to the fact how WebPack might resolve/initialize dependencies (e.g. by using eval in some cases) there is no guarantee that currentScript will point to the file which can be launched as worker. I think almost always this is the URL pointing to the main entry point of the application (not the library). 

> Also look at your code, you have script type and wait import.meta.url will work (we don't polyfill your code)? You can install the latest version of webpack and use:

I will give the setting a try to see how it behaves. But it is also a tricky situation: I am the author of `mylib` (actually `@coderline/alphatab`) which comes through NPM. My library is rather independent from the environment it will be used in. It ships an UMD (.js) and an ESM (.mjs) flavor. The ESM version could skip the `document.currentScript` but it's just there from the common typescript codebase. 

It's hard to convince all the integrators of my library to use a pre-release webpack to use my library in their projects. 

Also the polyfill approach would be maybe working if I am the author of the application using WebPack. I could write some wrappers, polyfills etc. which point to the correct location (even hard code it if required). But this is not a feasable solution for the market.
author: Danielku15

body:
I think I get closer to an understanding on how WebPack works and I might be able to ask now differently in regards to my needs that would help me propose a solution to my users. Let's ignore for now the previous discussion we had and focus on this new setup. 

Assume following setup: We have an application with two entry points: `app.js` and `worker.js` where both might import various dependencies which might be put into chunks by WebPack. We want to launch from `app.js` a new WebWorker using `worker.js` as entry point using inline workers. Inline workers are important for CORS purposes as WebWorkers have quite strict rules. 

Here live examples.

## Using import statements in worker.js 
Source Code: https://github.com/danielku15/webpack-worker/tree/import-statement

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/674916/154815816-31eba7d1-dbd6-4f16-87cf-925e55a4db7f.png)

The html-webpack-plugin ensures that all JS files are included as script tag to the index.html and therefore app.js can start. 
But when we start a new WebWorker using inline workers with `importScripts` we have no possibility in app.js to generate `importScripts` statements for all required files. Also WebPack itself does not take any care to load all the chunks and dependencies the worker.js might have. Therefore `worker.js` will never be actually started but is stuck in a wait state forever because "somebody" will ever provide the dependencies. How could this be solved?

## Using import functions in worker.js
Source Code: https://github.com/danielku15/webpack-worker/tree/import-function
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/674916/154816124-89a9a97e-2b7d-41fc-9181-ab2d958d0d91.png)

When using `import('./mylib.js').then(...)` WebPack generates some different code. It tries to generate a `script` tag on the page to automatically load the dependency. But generating script tags in web workers will fail for obvious reasons. The import function seems not to be supported in WebWorkers, it assumes a browser UI context with a DOM available. 

Side note: I would still have to test how this would behave if `mylib.js` has further dependencies which need to be loaded, or if `mylib.js` is split into multiple chunks. But for now let's keep it simple.
author: Danielku15

body:
I started now developing an own WebPack plugin and learned quite a lot on how some internals of WebPack work. While looking at the built-in plugins and hidden magic functions of WebPack I was able to identify all the bits I need to get a correct setup as I need. My intermediate solution is: 

1. In my library I added a detection for WebPack like: `typeof __webpack_require__ !== 'function'` to spawn workers compatible with a WebPack setup. There might be better/different ways to detect whether my library is embedded into WebPack and maybe I'll still change this. 
2. I trigger WebPack to generate a WebWorker wrapper code by spawning my workers with: `new Worker(new URL('@coderline/alphatab', import.meta.url))` where `@coderline/alphatab` is my library that can be resolved by WebPack. This wrapper code contains the WebPack bootstrapping, and even loads any dependency chunks using `importScripts`. This currently leads to a "0.js" being generated while I guess it should be a proper hash code, might report a more dedicated bug for that. 

The only problem with this solution is: CORS in cases CDNs are used. I am closing this question in favor of the discussion over at https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/14066 which is about the support of inline Workers.
author: Danielku15

body: @alexander-akait Unfortunately it is hard to search for issues in GitHub related to a "0.js" output. Are you aware of any existing issue that WebPack generates a "0.js" file name for the WebWorker wrapper in case you specify a whole library like described above? Otherwise I would enter a new one.
author: Danielku15

body:
I just faced a decently similar issue to this.

I am producing a React component library which is bundled / published with Rollup, but developed on top of and consumed downstream by react-scripts. Therefore I wanted this to be zero-config for react-scripts (Webpack 5), with the Worker published to do smart edge routing (expensive for main thread).

This guide is actually pretty helpful, but obviously doesn't explicitly address this case: https://webpack.js.org/guides/web-workers/

My solution had a few parts:
* First, I had to compile my worker to a separate file in the publish. I added an extra rollup config for just the worker.
* Second, based on NODE_ENV, I would either use the relative path to the worker in DEV, which react-scripts/Webpack would be able to resolve cleanly:
  * DEV: `new Worker(new URL('./worker.ts', import.meta.url))`
    * The worker is inside the component folder; it uses relative-only imports and avoids barrel modules. Webpack knows how to make this into a worker in dev, Rollup required a small amount of work.
  * PROD/publish: `new Worker(new URL(`${process.env.EDGEROUTER_RELATIVE_PATH_FROM_BUNDLE}`, import.meta.url))`
    * I inject this variable during publish, and it ends up looking like `./workers/edge-router.worker.js`.
    * In the outputted bundle you get in node_modules, for instance, the file `edge-router.worker.js` is a zero-dependency file (everything inlined), and the module consuming it loads the worker via `new Worker(new URL('./workers/edge-router.worker.js', import.meta.url))`
  * Pay careful attention to this line in that guide ^: `"Warning Using a variable in the Worker constructor is not supported by webpack ...  This is because webpack cannot analyse the syntax statically"`
  * So, you have to make sure that you do the injection like I did above directly into the `new URL` call, not a variable
* Finally you can just use the library downstream! Decently graceful, just very opaque while stumbling around.
   
Some caveats:
* This worker is actually used by one of the components in the library. I imagine you could summon it yourself with `new Worker(new URL(`@my/component-library/dist/workers/edge-router.worker.js`, import.meta.url))`, but I haven't tried of course
* This approach is probably coupled to Webpack/react-scripts 5, but I imagine with some custom config you could use it in other contexts

Not sure if this has value for you, but it was interesting for me. Performs beautifully too.

body:
FWIW, I have also brought back a (potentially more informative) message using the [compiler.hooks.invalid hook](https://webpack.js.org/api/compiler-hooks/#invalid). Something along the lines of ``Invalidated ${filename}`` printed to log. This acts both as a "Compiling..." message, and also helps identify errant recompiles due to other tools potentially modifying files behind your back.

IMO, this is also the best hook too capture the full rebuild time (changeTime - end of done hooks), to account for delay between `invalid` and `watchRun`

body:
It looks like this code was introduced in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/11529/ by @sokra.  Could you comment on the expected behaviour of this change?

Specifically, I'm wondering if it is really necessary that the existing chunk be found in every single ancestor path of every selected chunk?  That seems very restrictive (but maybe it is indeed necessary, I don't know).

I have a change that ensures that the existing chunk is just _an_ ancestor of every selected chunk.  This assumes that all parent chunks must be loaded before the child chunk.  However, I'm not confident that's always true...

Maybe this needs to be refined to do a separate check for each distinct entry point, if a selected chunk has multiple entry points as ancestors?

Would appreciate some guidance here.  Thanks!
author: scameron

body: "This assumes that all parent chunks must be loaded before the child chunk." [IIRC](https://medium.com/webpack/webpack-4-code-splitting-chunk-graph-and-the-splitchunks-optimization-be739a861366), the parent-child relationships only exist among chunk **groups**. On the other hand, it also sounds sub-optimal to ensure that one chunk is loaded because most of the time you might eventually load a lot of chunks (from parent chunk groups) of your projects.
author: nyngwang

body:
This is just my guess:

The purpose of splitting a new chunk is to de-duplicate the same chunk of code. But for every chunk in `selectedChunks`, you need to make sure that things can still work after the de-duplication. In the section of code you quoted, we have found a chunk `existingChunk` which has a name that fits one `cacheGroup` schema, but we have to check what I have said.

This part is my guessing:

To do so we have to make sure that `existingChunk` is in every `chunkGroup` for all `selectedChunks`, because we don't want any other dependent `chunkGroup`s to misfunction after the de-duplication.

On the other hand, given that a chunk can be in many `chunkGroup`s, we don't know which `chunkGroup` will be chosen for the final state (I guess that only one `chunkGroup` will be used for every chunk). To cover all cases, every `chunkGroup` of every `selectedChunks` is added into the `queue`. By the same reasoning, we have to add all parents of a `chunkGroup` if `existingChunk` is missing from it, because we don't know which `chunkGroup` will be used to cover the current chunk.

To conclude,

1. If "Isn't belonging to one parent enough?" is correct, then line 7 in the quoted snippet, i.e. `for (const group of chunk.groupsIterable) {`, also needs to be fixed, because it adds all `chunkGroup` for every `selectedChunks`.
2. "I'm wondering if that continue should actually be a break instead?" might not be a solution, since you might skip all `chunkGroup`s from some `selectedChunks`.
author: nyngwang

body:
All of this hinges on the question about the meaning of the parent-child relationship in chunk groups.  Is it true that _all_ parent chunk groups must be loaded before the child chunk group is loaded?  Or is it possible that only parent chunk group can be loaded before the child chunk group?

I have a feeling it's the actually the latter and my original assumption was wrong.  I've never seen this written down anymore, but thinking about it more, it does make some sense.  In that case, my idea doesn't work at all.
author: scameron

body: It's a great idea to think about whether something is necessary to optimize an existing tool further. But I think now the author of webpack is busy building something else so I'm afraid that we are the ones who need to test the idea ourselves. Currently, I don't have time to dive into the source code.

body:
I have rebuilt the app using the original configuration I provided and now it magically works... Git shows no changes at all.  
No console errors, everything works as expected. I am so confused. It turns out that just saying about an issue to other can solve it.  

Regardless, I am happy that it works now and thanks :D

body: If I understand your problem well, you can use [jquery from cdn](https://releases.jquery.com/) and set it as [external](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/externals/#externals) in webpack.
author: vankop

body:
Maybe I'll try to explain it again.
I first coded a program that ran with electron without problems and now I want to transfer it to webpack. My result should be an .exe. To achieve this I wanted to use Electron Forge, but probably only webpack should do it.
My log.html should be a login window, which leads to the wizard.html by pressing the login button. In my code without webpack I created an index.html, which first loads all the html files and then shows the log.html into the created main-container by `$('#page_log').show();`. By pressing the login button, the log.html first hides everything using `$('.main-container').hide();` and then finally loads the wizard.html.
So my question now is, can I get this javascript code to run or do I need to code the navigation differently?
author: V-Krause
V-Krause
libla

author: libla

body: it looks like it is some network problems, not sure how this related to webpack..
author: vankop

body: It might be unrelated to webpack--I'm not sure what determines how the chunks are loaded. If it were just a network error, though, I would assume that clearing cache would not fix it--that is, I wouldn't expect a bad result to go into cache as a result of a network error.
author: karptonite

body: it looks like there is no tls connection ( maybe bad network ). in protocol column there is no protocol, this means http request did not happened
author: vankop

body: Right, but in the same HAR file, many requests did happen. And the problem persisted (for that one chunk only, I think) until the user cleared cache. If it were just a bad network, that is not what I'd expect. And, of course, there is the issue that this problem has been reported by at least half a dozen Firefox users (and only Firefox). If it were just the result of a bad network, I would expect it to affect all browsers.
author: karptonite

body: sorry, there is no webpack related thing.. webpack uses jsonp (insert script tag on the page) to load chunks, so nothing to do from webpack side
author: vankop

body: Hi, we have the same issue, especially with Firefox, the amount of these errors that are happing on Firefox is extremely high in non proportional way to our Firefox users (nor to the amount of Firefox users world wide)

body:
Thanks, it works.

But it's difficult to control when you have external packages. In most cases you don't know what exactly files must be included in sideEffects and the single way is detect if such classes/imports are in use during the webpack-compilation. I understand that difficult to control polyfills (or extensions of global types) but checking if classes are in use or not it should be easy... 

Do you know is webpack going to tree-shake such cases automatically ? It's really important. It will help to reduce bundle-sizes in twice and more

body:
Thanks for the thorough explanation!

Now, the stylesheet is output as an asset.
https://github.com/TomokiMiyauci/mapcss/blob/beta/webpack/mod.js

I am not explaining it well enough, 
but I am assuming that the output CSS will eventually be inlined in the HTML head tag, or used as CSS Modules.

In other words, I intended to be able to use it as CSS Modules with `css-loader` or to output it inline with `style-loader`.

Therefore, I will need the following import statement.

```ts
import "map.css"
```

or

```ts
import { block } from "map.css"
```

"map.css" does not actually exist, but it scans content and generates virtual modules before the `css-loader`, etc.

Any opinions on this approach?
author: TomokiMiyauci

body: no, this approach will not work. webpack construct module graph from source modules. In your case you want to make module dependent on result of compilation.. so there is no way to do this since compilation depends on module graph..
author: vankop

body:
I know exactly what you mean.

In fact, there is no need to refer to the module graph, I can refer to the file directly.
In the end, it is important for me when and how to acquire the content in question.

I have at least two options.

- ReadFile all the target content myself at some point, such as immediately after `apply`; at HMR, readFile only the updated files.
- Prepare a placeholder in the module and do string substitution afterwards.

The latter is the approach being taken with [this project](https://github.com/unocss/unocss/blob/main/packages/webpack/src/index.ts#L61), but it seems pretty hacky.

The former seems to have a performance impact, but is this a bad practice?

body:
Just a hint which might be the reason: Windows uses by default `
` as line endings and with git on windows it might convert the `
` within the Git Repository to `
` on the working directory. This would explain why the hashes are different. Have you tried configuring your repo to keep consistent line endings for your files so that you have still `
` in your files within a Windows working directory? 

https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/getting-started-with-git/configuring-git-to-handle-line-endings

I faced exactly this issue here in the WebPack repo because files are with `
` and prettier complains constantly about `
` that I remove the ``. I added locally the git attributes for all js files to work around this.
author: Danielku15

body:
Hi @Danielku15,
this is one of the first things we checked. It is true that the line-endings may differ on windows and this becomes apparent if we check the checksums of the source files on windows vs. linux. They are indeed all different. Yet the issue described above only pertains to a very few chunks. In fact in the given example you can modify the code to not include the monaco-editor and the chunks will be identical regardless of the different line-endings/hashes on the different platforms. I've just done that in a branch called `without-monaco` in the repo above. Instead of the monaco editor there is a simple component that is lazily loaded in order to force a chunk to be created. The resulting chunks have indentical hashes on both platforms.

chunks on windows if monaco is not used:
```
15.03.2022  01:52    <DIR>          .
15.03.2022  01:52    <DIR>          ..
15.03.2022  01:52               501 130.1d06e52a0e4e1f5137e9.chunk.js
15.03.2022  01:52               324 88.f545f69adb5e4698fe5b.chunk.js
15.03.2022  01:52               188 index.html
15.03.2022  01:52           142.568 main.8081bffd652cc80daebb.js
15.03.2022  01:52               971 main.8081bffd652cc80daebb.js.LICENSE.txt
               5 File(s)        144.552 bytes
               2 Dir(s)  36.108.701.696 bytes free
```
and the same on linux:

```
130.1d06e52a0e4e1f5137e9.chunk.js
88.f545f69adb5e4698fe5b.chunk.js
index.html
main.8081bffd652cc80daebb.js
main.8081bffd652cc80daebb.js.LICENSE.txt
```
Plus, we know that the order of the some attributes differs in the generated files. I know that contenthash refers to content before it is being transpiled but I wouldn't be surprised if the imported files that make up the content of chunk are resolved or become available in a different order and cause this problem.

body:
Agree, the documentation does not explain:

- For a given module, can it match multiple rules, or just one?
- If just one, which one matches first, the first declared or the last declared?
- If multiple, in which order are they applied?

Note, this is NOT the same question as loader order, which is well defined; this is a question of _rule order_, which is left to the imagination.
author: Bilge

body:
FWIW I asked Copilot, and it says multiple rules can match (unless you use `oneOf`), and they are applied from top to bottom. But it's AI so YMMV lol.

>When multiple rules match a module in webpack, all matching rules are applied in the order they are defined in the configuration. This means the first matching rule is applied first, followed by the second, and so on.
author: Bilge

body: Empirically I have confirmed Copilot was actually correct.

body:
@alexander-akait  Not sure if I understand you. What other headers?

There's no compression nor uncompression needed as the browser knows it's a `svgz` file which needs gzip uncompression if we set these header. That's the only thing I need. This is working fine when done in .htaccess for production, I only need to set these two things for the `svgz` files in dev server config. Why is this so difficult to do? Or isn't it?

Reason this is also needed in dev mode is because Javascript always loads the files with `svgz` extension in front-end. Which only work when these are set to the right type and gzip encoding to let the browser understand it's an `svgz` file to automatically uncompress it and serve it as just a regular svg file the the frontend after auto uncompressing.

Right now I have some lazy workaround which tests the location to see if port is 8080 to know it should load the svg instead, but it's a pretty shitty and unstable workflow if something changes and I don't want different javascript code for development mode for obvious reasons. I also don't want to keep these svg files on the server. Only the svgz files.

So nothing compression needed, only needed AFAIK is two little things: set the content-type to 'image/svg+xml' and set the content-encoding to gzip for svgz files. The browser does the rest.

I would be surprised if this should take so much effort to do in devserver. I would expect 2 lines of code. What more code do you mean?
author: Friksel

body:
If you don't need something special why not use `static` (we use https://github.com/expressjs/serve-static under the hood)

>Which only work when these are set to the right type and gzip encoding to let the browser understand it's an svgz file to automatically uncompress it and serve it as just a regular svg file the the frontend after auto uncompressing.

Browsers don't uncompress svgz, your server does it, you just prepare files (adding `.*z`) to avoid overhead on server side due compressing time
author: alexander-akait

body:
> If you don't need something special why not use `static` (we use https://github.com/expressjs/serve-static under the hood)

Alright, so basically you're saying we just cannot set headers to files within the devserver config? I'm very surprised.
I'll take a look at that serve-static. Bummer that it's yet another lib to dive into for just setting file headers, but if that's the way  I'll take a look at it. Thanks for the tip.

> Browsers don't uncompress svgz, your server does it, you just prepare files (adding `.*z`) to avoid overhead on server side due compressing time

You aren't serious here right? Of course the server is NOT uncompressing svgz files. It wouldn't make any sense if a server would uncompress. The whole point of [svgz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scalable_Vector_Graphics#Compression) and gzip in general is to be able to use compressed files over the line to make files transmit faster. And that's also why we need to set the content-encoding type to gzip.

Sorry, but it's starting to look like you don't understand the svgz file format and gzip in general. I am surprised about that as your title says 'Collaborator'. So probably you made a typo somewhere. 

No offence, but at least now it's clear we're not on the same wavelength here and we're having miscommunication.
Thanks for your effort to help tho and have a nice day!

body:
> Lots of modules duplicated across many chunks
> Too many (relatively) small chunks

when using http2 this is not a problem (mostly). you should control this by using https://webpack.js.org/plugins/split-chunks-plugin/#splitchunksmaxasyncrequests https://webpack.js.org/plugins/split-chunks-plugin/#splitchunksmaxinitialrequests 

> Too many (relatively) small chunks

you can set min size https://webpack.js.org/plugins/split-chunks-plugin/#splitchunksminsize

For long-term caching you can use https://webpack.js.org/plugins/split-chunks-plugin/#splitchunkslayer , module layer could be set in `module.rules`
author: vankop

body:
Hi @vankop,

Whether or not many small requests matters or not with http2 depends a lot on the number/size of the requests.  With smaller or mid-size apps, it won't be a problem at all, as you say.  But with really large apps it definitely can make a difference (and it measurably does for our app).  The best analysis of this I've seen is in these two articles:

- https://medium.com/@asyncmax/the-right-way-to-bundle-your-assets-for-faster-sites-over-http-2-437c37efe3ff
- https://blog.khanacademy.org/forgo-js-packaging-not-so-fast/

But theory aside, in practice we do observe a difference in our benchmarks.

Regarding the suggestion to change configuration options like maxAsyncRequests and minSize, I've of course played around with every possible setting I could find, but nothing (so far) has come close to what we had before.

The biggest problem of all is just the complexity of the combinations when the optimizer runs against a big code base.  There's so much internal magic, it's really hard to know what affects what.  I end up just more or less trying semi-random combinations of things and hoping for something good to happen.  But with all the possible config options plus if you introduce cache groups and/or layers into the picture, the combinations are essentially endless.

There's something to be said for simplicity...  maybe `CommonsChunkPlugin` wasn't so sophisticated but it was easy to understand and control and debug.  I've been fighting with SplitChunks, on an off, for about 8 months now and still haven't produced an output I'm that happy with.

body:
import specifiers should be sorted alphabetically by esm spec..
so in case
a.js
```js
export const b = 2;
export const a = 1;
```
b.js
```js
export * as b from "./a"
```

index.js
```js
import {b} from "./b"
console.log(Object.keys(b)) // should print ['a','b']
```

body: Update: Looks like Babel isn't in play for anything in `node_modules` so this should just be about Webpack's handling of inline `require` in `.js` files, in v5 vs v4.
author: daniel-ac-martin

body: Changing the `require` to an inline `import()` causes the code to be transformed to a `__webpack_require__`, albeit wrapped in a promise.
author: daniel-ac-martin

body:
Hoisting the inline `import()` to a normal `import` at the top of the file, resolves the problem.

But I have no idea if such a patch would be acceptable to the upstream, as they may have made the `require` inline for a reason.

body:
Found the solution! 🙌

In my `tsconfig.json` in my `config` project (the one I run Webpack from), I had path aliases defined which I imported into the `webpack.config.dev.ts`. Obviously Webpack does not know about these path aliases unless I specifically define those in the `alias` section.

So my `tsconfig.json` is
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "noFallthroughCasesInSwitch": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "incremental": true,
    "downlevelIteration": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "rootDir": "../",
    "baseUrl": "."
    "paths": {
      "webpack-config": ["./config"]
    }
  },
  "include": ["./typescript", "./webpack", "../frontend"]
}
```

Then, in my `apps.webpack.config.ts` I have a section for all the aliases which I use for my apps/webpack
```ts
export const aliases: NonNullable<WebpackConfiguration["resolve"]>["alias"] = {
  webpack-config: resolve(__dirname),
  ...
};
```

Hope this helps!
Thank you @vankop for the very quick response! 🙌

body:
Hey @alexander-akait,

I struggled to get a minimal repro together, since I'm not totally sure what causes webpack to run into this issue, but I think I was able to narrow down where the issue is popping up. Here in `ConcatenatedModule`: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/2738eebc7880835d88c727d364ad37f3ec557593/lib/optimize/ConcatenatedModule.js#L905-L907

It seems like for some of the `references` that have the same `sourceOrder`, falling back to the original order in the `references` array is nondeterministic. When I changed that line to this (very hacky/gross, just trying to get something that would be deterministic so I could try to narrow down the issue), the problem went away:
```
references.sort(
	concatComparators(
		bySourceOrder,
		(a, b) =>
			a.connection.dependency.loc.start.line < b.connection.dependency.loc.start.line
				? -1
				: b.connection.dependency.loc.start.line < a.connection.dependency.loc.start.line
				? 1
				: a.connection.dependency.loc.start.column < b.connection.dependency.loc.start.column
				? -1
				: b.connection.dependency.loc.start.column < a.connection.dependency.loc.start.column
				? 1
				: 0,
		keepOriginalOrder(references),
	),
);
```

It's definitely a bug – I'm getting the issue without any plugins added. I was able to get a minimal repro together here: https://github.com/pgoldberg/concatenate-modules-bug.

It seems that there's an issue related to re-exporting within a package's `index.js`.
author: pgoldberg

author: pgoldberg

body:
After looking through a tiny bit more, I think the bug might be upstream of the `ModuleConcatenationPlugin` in the `SideEffectsFlagPlugin`. My guess is somewhere in here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/2738eebc7880835d88c727d364ad37f3ec557593/lib/optimize/SideEffectsFlagPlugin.js#L243-L314

Turning `optimization.sideEffects` off also seems to stop the bug – also seems to point to the problem being somewhere in the side effects logic
author: pgoldberg

body: I'll take a look
author: vankop

body: Thank you! I looked a little closer and it seems like the `SideEffectsFlagPlugin` expects [the `modules` received from the `optimizeDependencies` compilation hook](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/2738eebc7880835d88c727d364ad37f3ec557593/lib/optimize/SideEffectsFlagPlugin.js#L237) to have a deterministic ordering, but the order seems to change in the minimal repro. I dug a bit deeper and got a little lost, but I saw that `onDependenciesSorted` can be called in a different order here, which might be the root of the problem: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/2738eebc7880835d88c727d364ad37f3ec557593/lib/Compilation.js#L1463. Seems like it's maybe some kind of race condition?
author: pgoldberg

body:
Looking a little more, it seems like `handleModuleCreation` is always called in the correct order for the dependencies of `dep/index.js` ([here](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/2738eebc7880835d88c727d364ad37f3ec557593/lib/Compilation.js#L1476)), but then the callbacks for `factorizeModule` on those dependencies sometimes get called in a different order ([here](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/2738eebc7880835d88c727d364ad37f3ec557593/lib/Compilation.js#L1774-L1880)), which then causes `inner_dep2` to get processed before `inner_dep`.

`dep/index.js`, `inner_dep`, and `inner_dep2` are all referring to the minimal repro, for context
author: pgoldberg

body: @pgoldberg Can you try to set https://webpack.js.org/configuration/other-options/#parallelism to `1` and check? If you think it is race it should help, and it means we have a bug somewhere...
author: alexander-akait

body:
Just did it with the minimal repro and it does seem like the issue goes away – looks like it must be a race condition. After spending some time debugging with the test repo, it seems like it's a race condition with `NormalModuleFactory.create` for `inner_dep2/index.js` finishing before `inner_dep/index.js` here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/2738eebc7880835d88c727d364ad37f3ec557593/lib/Compilation.js#L1973-L2033

That's the first place I see the order become incorrect
author: pgoldberg

body: So let's open an issue fir this, sounds like a bug

author: vankop

body:
Hi @vankop!

Thanks for the answer! 🙌

Using https://statoscope.tech/ definitely brought some light into the dark. Since then I've done some intensive tests to try finding out what's going on. So far I found that:
- the issue does not occur from **path aliases**
- it has nothing to do with the **monorepo structure**

**BUT** it seems that webpack has somehow problems with `.tsx` files! As soon as I export more than one TSX component from an `index.ts` **ALL** the sources within that index get bundled! I'm pretty sure this is some kind of misconfig, right?

Just to be clear here - assume the following  `App.tsx` _(which is an import in the entry file `index.ts`)_
```ts
// App.tsx - types skipped for better readability
import { Test } from 'packages/test';

export const App = () => {
  return <Test />;
}
```

The "package" test looks like:
```
test/
  Test.tsx
  Test1.tsx
  testFunc.ts
  index.ts
```
where `index.ts` is
```ts
// index.ts
export { Test } from './Test';
export { Test1 } from './Test1';
export { testFunc } from './testFunc'
```

The **app bundle** with the above example includes `Test` AND `Test1`! I would expect it to only include `Test` since `Test1` is not in use. However, the more `.tsx` files I add and export from the `index.ts`, the more components get added to the bundle.

If I have only `.ts` files, I have no issue! Then only the source I am using inside `App.tsx` gets added to the bundle.

This might be a very stupid question, so again any push into the right direction welcome! 🙌
author: Poolshark

body: you should use [sideEffects flag](https://webpack.js.org/guides/tree-shaking/#clarifying-tree-shaking-and-sideeffects) to make this work, also make sure that this code doesn't transpiles to commonjs
author: vankop
Poolshark

body:
> But the import via the index.ts causes all files to be included in the bundle.

As I said you need to set sideEffects flag correctly **or** result of ts transpiling is commonjs this prevents tree-shaking. You can try to create small reproducible repo I will take a look
author: vankop

body:
Hey!

I have played around with the `sideEffects` flag but so far nothing did the job. Guess just setting it to `false` does not do the trick.

Anyway, I have managed to create a [minimal example](https://github.com/Poolshark/webpack-minimal-example) from my (quite complex) monorepo, which actually shows the problem. I hope this works for you!

@vankop I really appreciate the time you spending helping me out here! Thank you so much! 🙌

PS.: Let me know if the minimal example is sufficient.
author: Poolshark

body: you should set `optimization.sideEffects=true` https://github.com/Poolshark/webpack-minimal-example/blob/master/config/webpack/config/apps.webpack.ts#L107 this will enable side effects plugin on webpack side. _____ This should help
author: vankop

body:
Hi!

Thank you very much! This solved the problem! 🙌

It's just very weird that I had to set `sideEffetcs: false` in the `package.json` of the frontend package and `sideEffects: true` in `optimization.sideEffects` of. the Webpack config but I guess this is how it is.

Anyway, thanks so much for your help!

body:
I've tried to reproduce this behavior in minimal example, but it works as it should, seems like problem has gone.
Maybe i've done something wrong in my previous attempt.

Here's implementation of my idea: <https://github.com/NEWESTERS/module-federation-example>.
There is package named `library` with its own webpack config.
Source code of `library` uses webpack's internal variable `__webpack_init_sharing__`, which will be replaced by webpack on `library` build script.
Then i run webpack in `host` application, that depends on `library`, and that means that `library` source code went through webpack twice.
## TL;DR
So abstract version of my question is: "What happens, if source code, that contains webpack's  internal variables, goes through webpack twice (__source code__ -> __webpack__ -> __build-1__ -> __webpack__ -> __build-2__)".

经过我运行你的项目 我发现在经过compile函数的时候 获取的webpackconfig 配置是空的 进而报错。 当我修改完毕后 你的webpack.config.client文件的配置 传递给webpack函数 配置会报错 现在没有具体发现是哪一行。 但是你上边贴图的错算是解决了 。 因此对于你上边的图报错 原因就是 传递给webpack参数为{}  。

After I run your project, I find that the ‘webpackConfig’ parameter obtained when I pass the compile function is empty, and then an error is reported. When I finish modifying your webpack config. When the configuration of the client file is passed to the webpack function, an error will be reported. At present, no specific line of configuration has been found. But the mistake of your upper mapping is solved. Therefore, the reason for the error in the above figure is that the parameter passed to webpack is {}.

body:
> Here the blueMethod is only needed for the async chunk but is still added to the initial chunk.
Is there a way we can optimize such cases without refactoring the file into smaller files? may be by eliminating the exports not used by the particular chunk?

no, webpack can't optimize on code level (e.g. one exports in one chunk, other exports in another chunk), only on module level.. 

Also this optimization not always possible + this will require changes in how webpack bootstrap modules..
author: vankop

body:
probably you have something like
```
main.js
- 50b74e3a.chunk.js
```

so when your chunk changed, `main.js` hash also changed.. so you have 2 options:
- add hash in naming to `main.js` also ( recommended )
- prevent browser to cache `main.js` ( `Cache-control: no-cache` )

```

Making a request to that "busy" server, actually stay in pending and in rare case single response may be received but... due to that heavy busy state the express server can't do much more.

body:
After some digging, it could be related to https://github.com/nodejs/node-gyp/issues/2713 (also see this PR https://github.com/nodejs/node-gyp/pull/2721).

TLDR: New(er) versions of `node-gyp` create a symlink of Python at `./node_modules/{dependency}/build/build/node_gyp_bins/python3`. As far as I can tell, webpack tries to read the content and returns with `undefined` as the symlink isn't followed.

body:
I'm seeing this on Next.JS 14.2.16

One thing that I managed to do to mitigate the issue was changing the minified file javascript `node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js`:

Using a regular expression search on this file, I tried `[^.]_updateWithBuffer(\s*)\(([^0-9]\w)*\)(\s*)\{`.

Then, I changed the string found from `}_updateWithBuffer(v){` to `}_updateWithBuffer(v){if(v===undefined)return;`

Before:
```
...)$.copyWithin(0,v,L)}}_updateWithBuffer(v){const{exports:E,buffered:P,mem:R}=this;const $=v...
```

After:
```
...)$.copyWithin(0,v,L)}}_updateWithBuffer(v){if(v===undefined)return;const{exports:E,buffered:P,mem:R}=this;const $=v...
```

body:
`webpack-virtual-modules` can be a problem, when trying to create a hash, but for some reason our content is `undefined`, here the place - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/util/hash/wasm-hash.js#L42, I asked for the stack so I could see where it came from, but apparently it happens in the hook so the stack is dirty, https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/FileSystemInfo.js#L3582 - the main problem here is how to we work with directories, you can debug which directory is a problem, hashes are - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/FileSystemInfo.js#L3607 and https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/FileSystemInfo.js#L3613 

Can you debug? Maybe we should add `undefined` checks, but this may lead to the fact that the directory change will not be tracked by the timestamp. Need a little more information

Thank you! I think I have found the reason. Let me have some time doing some research about it.
author: darkyzhou

body:
Sorry for being late. There were two reasons:
* [antd-dayjs-webpack-plugin](https://github.com/ant-design/antd-dayjs-webpack-plugin). It will construct another `Compilation` after the first run due to its way of implementation, thus causing the second run.
* [fork-ts-checker-webpack-plugin](https://github.com/TypeStrong/fork-ts-checker-webpack-plugin). Using tsc to perform type checking, some symbolic files of the project will be modified somehow, causing webpack's `Watcher` to be notified, thus leading to the second run.
author: darkyzhou

author: alexander-akait

body:
If the shell is already authorized, id put that into a piece of state, like recoil, redux, react context - which is shared as a singleton so all other apps can see its authorized. 

The browser will respect origin policies - control security via CSP headers or whatever else. 
Security wise - not much different than any other application. Webpack uses chunk loading globals like window.webpackChunkLoad - so if someone hacks your site, they can take over the chunk loading global of webpack. Remote entry should pose no additional threats. If someone is already able to hijack anything in your application - you've already been hacked and federation is least of your concerns. 

Security best practices - same as you'd do to secure rogue JS running on your website. CSP policies so the browser is only allowed to accept resources from trusted domains.

Hey, I'm happy to put together a repo. I have one more observation to share before that could help identify the issue. My repo is based on this [angular-electron](https://github.com/maximegris/angular-electron) template.

They basically import externals through `window.require` [link to entire code](https://github.com/maximegris/angular-electron/blob/a3eeb6a953d3f178d68378c18478ae2e0fc02132/src/app/core/services/electron/electron.service.ts#L20-L26):

```typescript
    if (this.isElectron) {
      this.fs = window.require('fs');
      ....
```

So in order to call `fs` I need to go through `this.electronService.fs`.

If I instead use the import directly `import fs from 'fs';` in the top of my component, then I receive the error:

    `Module not found: Error: Can't resolve 'fs'`.

My app is an Electron app only, so I don't need dynamic imports. The app will never run in the browser. The `import from` worked with Webpack 4. I am not saying it's the culprit but it is certainly a candidate. I hope my description makes sense. Any help is highly appreciated!

> > I have already specified it in the code as a dynamic import that should not be transformed
>

> 
> Other bundlers do the same. Also you can have multiple `@ant-design/cssinjs` impots - dynamic and async, what we should do here if we should leave `import` as is? I just want to say the function notation provide the real power. If you need more arguments in callback for checks just provide infromation what will help you

I have the impression that @ntucker has the exact same question that I have in mind right now:
Is webpack able to leave certain module inclusions as-is?

Consider I have the following `.js` code:

```js
import { MyClass } from "./MyClass.js";
import { createRequire } from "node:module";
import rescape from "@stdlib/utils-escape-regexp-string"; // this is a commonjs module

const path = createRequire(new URL(".", import.meta.url))("node:path"); // explicitly include `node:path` using the `require`-mechanism
rescape(`${new MyClass()}`);
(await import("inquirer")).default([]);
```

Consider I want `inquirer`, `node:module`, `@stdlib/utils-escape/regexp-string` and `node:path` to be externals.
Is there some way for me to specify that `webpack` shouldn't touch the expressions I am using for including the modules?`

Meaning:
  - `./MyClass.js` should be bundled
  - `node:module` should be `import`ed
  - `@stdlib/utils-escape-regexp-string` should be `import`ed (commonjs are treated as if they were ESModules with `default` exports)
  - `node:path` should be `require`d using `createRequire`
  - `inquirer` should be dynamically imported

Is there some way for me to reach this goal without having to specify the nature of the inclusion expression for each module?
author: neurolag

body:
@alexander-akait 

I think you're assuming a web application target? Not library and not nodejs? If webpack should not support those, there shouldn't be [multiple ](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/externals/) [docs ](https://webpack.js.org/guides/author-libraries/) [pages ](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/target/#string) on them.

Otherwise, your comments don't make sense to me. 

> There are a lot of optimizations async modules via import(...), so you just lose them

Node: I don't consider hoisting a require() on an optional package to top level being an optimization. This means the first import will fail and *nothing* will work. It is in fact breaking, and defeats the entire purpose of using a dynamic import to begin with. Additionally, forcing parsing of all javascript up-front is an anti-optimization and increases startup times and memory usage.

Library: Diabling the ability of library consumers to use these optimizations *DISABLES* optimizations. So it is in fact better to keep these in place so they *can* be optimized properly.

> Other bundlers do the same

No rollup works fine and doesn't break this. However, I'd like to use webpack for everything as it has a better ecosystem and I can share things with my application bundler.

> what we should do here if we should leave import as is

I don't understand the nature of the question - you answered it yourself: 'leave import as is'. Obviously I'm not implying we should do this for web application bundling if that's what you're implying. [Externals](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/externals/) are definitely a thing in webpack.

> I just want to say the function notation provide the real power. If you need more arguments in callback for checks just provide infromation what will help you

Can you elaborate on what this is? Maybe there are docs links that explain more? I don't know what 'function notation' is in this context.

body:
Is there some way to flag a dependency as `external` without overwriting its `externalType`?

Consider the following code:

```ts
import { createRequire } from "node:module";
import puppeteer from "puppeteer"; // I want to import the ESM version of `puppeteer` here

const anotherPuppeteer = createRequire(new URL(".", import.meta.url)("puppeteer"); // At some other place - in another source file, by chance, I want to import the CommonJS version of `puppeteer`
```

Is there some way to tell webpack that `puppeteer` is externl and that the statements should be left untouched? (meaning the `import` should stay an `import`, the `require`-statement should stay a `require`-statement)

That would be a great feature as you probably want to import the module exactly as you stated it in your code.

What do you mean by `lose a lot of optimizations`
For example my initial chunk (entry point) have a different imports with node_modules. I have several options:
- default
- create global common vendor chunk for all entry points
- another custom cacheGroups  for vendors

Why when I have to load entry point A do I need to load common vendors for all entry points?

Example: 
- go to page A
- I want to load vendors for page A
- I want to load entry for page A
- another node_modules can be load asynchronously by async chunk

Example with bundle analyzer:
**Default(without cacheGroups)** - it's very bad splitting for big projects
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3369304/191086832-1a11e122-da71-4992-8831-70d6c5f36480.png)

**Vendor common** - better but not ideal
```js
vendorCommon: {
  chunks: 'initial',
  priority: 40,
  enforce: true,
  reuseExistingChunk: true,
  test: (module, ctx) => {
    if (!/[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/.test(module.resource)) return false
    const chunks = ctx.chunkGraph.getModuleChunksIterable(module)
    return chunks.size > 1
  },
  name: `vendors~common`,
},
```

With common vendor - 730kb(vendor) + 111kb(entry)


I want:

body:
Sorry, you don't want to listen to me, we are not esbuild, we have loader/plugin conceptions (where you can implement an logic/transformation/minification/rewritting/etc).

And I ask you not to poke me into the code, I perfectly understand what esbuild do there, but you do not want to read the documentation.

If you need content of file you need to create a loader and change generate code there (but what is code, no information in the question), if you need to modify resolving logic - you have `resolver`, if you want to provide externals - you have `externals`, need fallback to another module - `resovler.fallback`, these things are made for different purposes, yes, in some places they can do the same things.

> How to match such files in webpack? They do not necessarily come from node_ Modules is a custom name, and then the content is written later after matching. This is easy to do in esbuild, but what about webpack? It will first report an error, saying that 'vemix: manifest' is not a valid format.

What should I understand from your question? You didn't specify what you want to do - provide content, do fallback, do another resolve, rewrite path, generate runtime? What?


```
function ({context, request}, callback) {
      console.log(request); // no no no not this, but the content of the file, not the request path !!!
      callback(null, request);
    }
```

Why not use `fs.readFile`, we provide all information to do any things. If you need plugin - you can register own schema and create a handler, but it will recreate more code than `externals`.

Also you can create `mock/vemix_manifest.js` and specify it in `externals`.

There are a lot of possible solutions for your problem, If you want a full answer, then please provide a real use case and a problem - what will be in `vemix:` (and other schemas), what we should do with `node:fs` (provide fs or do mock), what is your target (web/node/deno/etc).

body:
`sideEffects: 'flag'` 

> To only use the manual flag and do not analyse source code:
> The 'flag' value is used by default in non-production builds.

With `'flag'` webpack skips analyze code (i.e. only `package.json` flags used), so it is faster, you need to use `true` in the `production` node
author: alexander-akait

body: However, if you set true in the production mode, a library is used In json, sideEffects: false is used. The final result will still be based on the sideEffects declared by the library, and the side effects of the library will be deleted. I want to know whether this is the default behavior of the webpack or a bug of the webpack. Optimization is reasonable After sideEffects is set to true, I understand whether webpack will analyze the source code of the library and output the side effect code to the bundle, instead of still following the sideEffects field of package.json of the library

body:
I don't think it is the bug, all works as expected, 

When you have `"sideEffects": true` our output:
```
(()=>{"use strict";console.log("hi")})();
``` 

i.e. we keep module because it has side effect

But if you set `"sideEffects": false` our output is empty, because webpack knows - no side effects and can drop the whole module (and nested modules)

body:
@alexander-akait,
Would you happen to know what could cause build files to differ from a build to another when nothing is modified in the source files or configs ?
author: semiaddict

body:
Sounds like you have racing somewhere, so in one case you have loaded module and no problems, in another case no. How often do you see this error? Is there any specific steps? How extensions for CKEditor is loading (maybe you miss `await`/`Promise` somewhere)? 

Is it possible to create small example (I know it can be not easy)? Also I see `regeneratorRuntime`, do you use babel (can you try to update, maybe bug in babel)?
author: alexander-akait

body:
Thank you @alexander-akait for you time.

> How often do you see this error?

I very often end up having to rebuild 3-4 times before getting it right.
So I would say over 60% of the builds are corrupt.

> Is there any specific steps?

No, not really. 
I currently check the loading of each dynamic chunk after each build manually by opening/viewing (usually via a tab change or a click on a link) the vue component that is responsible for loading the chunk to see if it loads fine.
The same exact process produces errors on most builds, but at some point a build makes all chunks load fine.

> How extensions for CKEditor is loading (maybe you miss `await`/`Promise` somewhere)?

I am integrating CKEditor and its plugins [from source](https://ckeditor.com/docs/ckeditor5/latest/installation/advanced/alternative-setups/integrating-from-source.html) and sing the [CKEditorWebpackPlugin](https://github.com/ckeditor/ckeditor5-dev) to extract translations.
Can a missing `await` in the source code actually affect the build process? Or are you speaking about missing `await` in the webpack config?

> Also I see `regeneratorRuntime`, do you use babel (can you try to update, maybe bug in babel)?

I do indeed use babel, which I just updated yesterday (along with all other dependencies) without any improvements in the chunk loading errors.

I will try to do that early next week, but I don't know if I will manage to actually reproduce on a small example (my application is quite large).
