body:
@tommypepsi, thank you for your response. I don't think I have any build specific variables that change, so it might not be related after all.

@alexander-akait, I managed to highly reduce my codebase and still reproduce the error.
It, however, occurs much less often with the reduced codebase, so I had to run the build script about 20 times to get a broken build.
Attached is an archive containing both a working build and a corrupt one.
You will notice that the files `metaScore.Editor.css`, `metaScore.vendors.lottie.js`, and `metaScore.vendors.lottie.js.map` are different in the two builds. The last two files are actually very different, with both having extra content appended to them.

[random-chunk-loading-errors.zip](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/files/9971167/random-chunk-loading-errors.zip)

I'm also attaching the reduced codebase in case this can help, but please note that you won't be able to actually test the build as it requires access to a CMS. You should however be able to run the build script and check if `metaScore.vendors.lottie.js` is malformatted.

body:
It seems that my custom `splitChunks.cacheGroups` setting was at cause here.
I thought I had already tried to remove it before, but now I seem to be able to build fine if I remove that setting.
I don't know what's wrong with it, but I can live without it :)

Thank you @alexander-akait and @tommypepsi for your help!
author: semiaddict

body: Users were intermittently getting chunk load errors in the Dev and Staging environments after deployments. I couldn't find any differences in the deployments when comparison to the Production environment, which has never had these errors. When viewing the calls in the DevTools, I could see 404 not found responses, but the files that were "not found", did exist in the Dist folder on the application server. My issue turned out to be security related. When comparing the list of accounts under site security between Production vs Dev and Staging, I noticed I had missed adding the general Users group to the sites on both the Dev and Staging sites. Adding the Users group to each site with default security resolved the chunk load errors for me.
author: rosychuk

body:
This sometimes happens when two versions of a module are installed, either globally or locally.
Are you sure it's loading the correct module (i.e. from the correct location)?

Why is it loading a module from a Temp folder?

Here are some related issues that might be of some help:

- https://github.com/babel/babel/issues/14576
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/58919016/babel-and-webpack-are-throwing-cant-resolve-regenerator-runtime-runtime
- https://github.com/bvaughn/react-window/issues/622
- https://bobbyhadz.com/blog/react-module-not-found-cant-resolve-babel-runtime
author: semiaddict

body:
I think I have a similar issue.

By default, with webpack 5 and `asyncWebAssembly: true`, webpack seems to generate some glue code that dynamically loads the wasm on module startup.

However, this seems to assume that the wasm is served from a certain location at the server. I would like to package this in a way that doesn't require the wasm to be served separately.

For example, this would be possible (I think) through inlining the WASM. Does WebPack support something like this?
author: matthiasgeihs

body: Do you mean handle WASM file as a file and load it by self?
author: alexander-akait

body: I mean something like `asyncWebAssemblyInline: true`. And then, when writing `import factorial from "factorial.wasm";`, WebPack generates code that inlines the WASM (like with `asset/inline`), and loads the WASM module from the inlined WASM (instead of trying to fetch it from the server). Does that make sense?
author: matthiasgeihs

body: Sorry for delay, you a loader for such purposes. Why you need this, because some optimizations are impossible in the such case
author: alexander-akait

body: We have been running into problems when importing the `wasm` package into other packages with other contexts. In these cases, there might be no webserver available that servers the wasm. Nevertheless, we, ideally, wanted to have 1 bundle that we can distribute everywhere.
author: matthiasgeihs

body:
I've been able to achieve this by packaging the WASM as `asset/inline` (as described [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/70420273/how-can-i-make-webpack-embed-my-wasm-for-use-in-a-web-worker)), but my solution requires some custom extra code to load the WASM.
```
import * as wasmLib from "./pkg";
import wasmDataURL from "./pkg/example.wasm";

const base64Data = (wasmDataURL as any).split("base64,")[1]; // Extract base64 string from DataURL.
const wasmBuffer = Uint8Array.from(atob(base64Data), (c) => c.charCodeAt(0)); // Convert base64 to Uint8Array.

wasmLib.initSync(wasmBuffer); // Init WASM library.
```

It would be cool if this glue code wasn't needed.
author: matthiasgeihs

body:
I think the problem is overlapping, when you have very small hash, webpack accidentally can change your code, for example:
```js
var myVar = 1234;
``` 
And when you have very small size of a hash we can change it to:
```
var myVar = 12ab; // Invalid number, but you can have `1234` contenthash in your assets
```

yeah it is very rare case, but it can happend, so my recommendation is `8`
author: alexander-akait

body: Got it, all my projects have been changed to 8. Thanks!
author: foyzhao

body: > I think the problem is overlapping, when you have very small hash, webpack accidentally can change your code, for example: > > ```js > var myVar = 1234; > ``` > > And when you have very small size of a hash we can change it to: > > ``` > var myVar = 12ab; // Invalid number, but you can have `1234` contenthash in your assets > ``` > > yeah it is very rare case, but it can happend, so my recommendation is `8` I checked part of the code of the plugin RealContentHashPlugin, and I am friends with foyzhao. So I debugged his code and got a hashMap like this Then the content is replaced in the RealContentHashPlugin So if it is replaced like this, there are several problems: 1.~As long as there is a hash at the beginning of a number, a syntax error will occur 2.~Related, as long as a certain js or css is changed or the vendor is changed in a passive chain, changes and new hashes will be generated. The above is the information I got from debugging, I hope it will help to fix or troubleshoot this problem

author: 2snEM6

body:
Hey,

**I ran into the same issue while upgrading an old repo to Webpack 5.**

At runtime, I was getting the following error:

```
ReferenceError: jsonpScriptSrc is not defined
```

After investigating, I discovered that the problem was caused by a dependency conflict in my `package.json`. Specifically, I had the `webpack-retry-chunk-load-plugin`, which had a peerDependency on Webpack 4.

To resolve it, I removed the plugin from my dependencies and from my production config (`webpack.prod.js`).

Here’s how it looked before the fix:

```js
new RetryChunkLoadPlugin({
  maxRetries: 3,
  retryDelay: 3000,
  // Optional: Add more specific configurations for RetryChunkLoadPlugin if needed
}),
```

After this change, the `jsonpScriptSrc` error disappeared. 
**I also confirmed by searching through the entire build folder, and the broken `jsonpScriptSrc` reference no longer appears.**

Hope this helps!

body:
> In process of building "filesystem" cache webpack hits the /home and root / directories and falls with EACCES error
PackFileCacheStrategy :: Error: Unable to snapshot resolve dependencies

It can happen when you have `import foo from "/test.js"`, i.e. absolute URLs, also resolver allows to change behaviour and set for example `modules` to root and webpack again will do it, if you need the real answer we need your configuration as minimum
author: alexander-akait
Smanst3r

body:
The reason behind that is 
https://github.com/webpack/enhanced-resolve/blob/main/lib/SymlinkPlugin.js
```
if (resolveContext.fileDependencies)
    resolveContext.fileDependencies.add(path);
```
fills _resolveContext.fileDependencies_ with array containing paths to very top of filesystem tree.
It looks like it is done deliberately, as there are a test case for that
https://github.com/webpack/enhanced-resolve/blob/main/test/dependencies.js

The bug is reproduce-able when you restrict the **/home** directory access for users, so it would look like that
`ls -lZ /`
`drwx--x--x. root root system_u:object_r:home_root_t:s0 home`

Not sure how to fix that and is it worth to..

Regards,

body:
Okay so I am seeing something here! Here's my debugging:
```
getFilenameFromUrl called
/main.c3a315512688edee7712.hot-update.json (url)
paths
[
  {
    outputPath: '/home/react_app/build',
    publicPath: '/services-node-44248'
  }
]
/main.c3a315512688edee7712.hot-update.json
urlObject
Url {
  protocol: null,
  slashes: null,
  auth: null,
  host: null,
  port: null,
  hostname: null,
  hash: null,
  search: null,
  query: null,
  pathname: '/main.c3a315512688edee7712.hot-update.json',
  path: '/main.c3a315512688edee7712.hot-update.json',
  href: '/main.c3a315512688edee7712.hot-update.json'
}
/main.c3a315512688edee7712.hot-update.json
publicPathObject
Url {
  protocol: null,
  slashes: null,
  auth: null,
  host: null,
  port: null,
  hostname: null,
  hash: null,
  search: null,
  query: null,
  pathname: '/services-node-44248',
  path: '/services-node-44248',
  href: '/services-node-44248'
}
```

It does not go into 
```
if (urlObject.pathname && urlObject.pathname.startsWith(publicPathObject.pathname))
```
because `urlObject.pathname` (/main.c3a315512688edee7712.hot-update.json) does not start with `publicPathObject.pathname` (/services-node-44248).

In the local version it does find it because `publicPathObject.pathname` is `'/'`.

Now, if the url is `/main.c3a315512688edee7712.hot-update.json` where is it getting the `/services-node-44248` path from? is it using the env variable `PUBLIC_URL`? I could proxy any request from `/PUBLIC_URL` to `/` but I really cannot, because it seems to conflict with the `/static` proxy rule. @alexander-akait any idea?

thanks for the help so far

body:
I still can't get it to work...  The root of the problem is:
- I need to proxy any request like `myserver/services-node-xxxxx/whatever` to `mydockerserver:xxxxx/whatever`
- Without any configuration, CRA  and webpack generate an html file where all paths are relative to `/`, so any static files are searched in `myserver/static` instead of `myserver/services-node-xxxxx/static`
- Now, there's the PUBLIC_URL option that makes the paths in the html apparently correct (using `PUBLIC_URL=/services-node-xxxxx/` urls in html are now `/services-node-xxxxx/static`). However, it doesn't only change the paths in the public files but also where they are served in the docker/app itself. So now, `myserver/services-node-xxxxx/whatever` is proxied to `mydockerserver:xxxxx/whatever` by nginx, so the requests reaches the app as `localhost:3000/whatever` but because of PUBLIC_PATH,  the app is actually serving things to `localhost:3000/services-node-xxxxx/whatever` so things are not correctly found.

The real problem then is, isn't there any webpack option similar to PUBLIC_URL that allows setting the actual public urls in public files but does not change how the app serves the files?
author: raquelhortab

body: it seems it could be enough if relative public_url were allowed since setting `publicPath: ''` in `node_modules/react-scripts/config/webpack.config.js` seems to fix the issue. The problem is I'd need an official workaround (through environment variables) rather than a patch...

body:
Thank you for looking at it!

author: jeffrson

body:
> How safe (or stable) is it, given I have to opt-in via "experiments"?

It is safe for most of cases, expect some edge cases, but you will see it while you development if you faced with such bugs (unlikely it will be).

UMD is such legacy things, and when you set `type: "module"` you are telling to Node.js - `my projects is ESM`, so everything will be considered from this side. I want to say it is not a ts/ts-loader/webpack bug. 

Even more - why do you bundle your library, because if you use only js/ts (and don't have side effects things like changing DOM, inject CSS and etc in top level scope) I recommend not to use bundler at all and keep your code in ESM, double bundling is lose many perfomance and your application can be bigger in this case
author: alexander-akait

body: Well, yes, unfortunately there are <name>.module.scss files imported from typescript for the components in lib-client that are not found by application if not bundled.
author: jeffrson

body:
@jeffrson Just intresting why you want to bundle `CSS`, you `embedded` your styles wtihout possible to rewrite. 

If you consider the code as a library, then this is not a good idea, you don’t know where it will be used and developer might want to rewrite them and there are a lot of other cases when something in styles will be need to change. In that case better to complie CSS separately and provide example in docs how to use it in bundle (so developer can use an own library or rewrite them).

If you still want to embed your CSS (widgets/external embed), you can use vanially JS and DOM/CSSOM API and it will be more portable.

Note - the first thing allow to your lbirary will be more optimized (and better tree shaking and not only), using the second solution you can make pure function as `/* PURE */` (no side effects, i.e. all functions which change DOM/inject CSS are **not** `pure`) and have good optimization too.
author: alexander-akait

Of course you're right regarding flexibility and optimizations! Indeed, I would prefer if we could change the lib to get rid of webpack in the libraries. I will discuss it with our frontend team. Thanks a lot!!!
author: jeffrson

body: Yeah, webpack is useful for bundling applications and optimize them, but now is 2022 and ESM works in most of modern browsers, most of libraries migrated (or start to migrate) to ESM, it will have many benefits, and therefore, when the developers builds their application, they themselves will decide which browsers and versions wnt to support and how inject dependecies and how optimize them, double bundling is not good idea at all, because you will lose a lot of optimizations

body:
I have encountered the same issue since upgrading to Typescript 4.9.4 with a issue that looks the same. Looking around issues I narrowed it down to these this regression:

It looks like Typescript 4.9.2+ changed the code that it emits for dynamic imports: the import path is assigned to a variable (see https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/51554) prior to passing it to the `import()` function. Therefore Webpack does not seem to understand what to import and emits `Critical dependency: the request of a dependency is an expression` warnings. Before v4.9.2 it does not emit any warnings and it works fine.

What's emitted by Typescript 4.9.4:
```await Promise.all(filesToImport.map((e) => { var _a; return _a = `./relative/path/to/files/${e}`, Promise.resolve().then(() => tslib_1.__importStar(__webpack_require__("./path/of/app sync recursive")(_a))); }))```

What's emitted by Typescript 4.9.1-beta (and earlier) with the same project and Webpack configuration:
```await Promise.all(filesToImport.map((e) => Promise.resolve().then(() => tslib_1.__importStar(__webpack_require__("./relative/path/to/files sync recursive ^\\.\\/.*$")(`./${e}`)))))```

The solution for me is to use Typescript 4.9.1-beta for the time-being, I tried using the dynamic import magic comments but they don't seem to influence much either. 

@alexander-akait is it a known issue that Typescript 4.9.2+ emits different code for dynamic imports? Or is this something that would not influence Webpack?

body: Resolved my own issue. The site appears to be redefining the global `self` variable between the point that the entry file is loaded and creates the chunk array on `self` and when the chunk actually loads. When the chunk loads, `self` is a completely new object not containing the original chunk array.

body:
Hi!

In my case `eval-source-map` was used by mistake. My point is that it took me forever to diagnose why `usedExports` did not work. It might be obvious for you that `devtool` setting can impact `usedExports`, but it definitely wasn't obvious for me, a simple webpack user. There was no diagnostic message telling me that I'm doing something weird. It was not clear from any of the documentation that I linked in my original comment. There are many conditions stated there that are required for `usedExports` to do its job, but correct `devtool` setting is not one of them.

Perhaps this issue is meant as a documentation improvement issue.

body:
You can't know when files will be changed when not, so the best solution is cache, look at code here https://github.com/webpack-contrib/html-minimizer-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.js#L398 you can use the same approach in your plugin, so you will have:
1. No extra work in the watch mode
2. Cache between compilation (if you will have filesystem cache your plugins will be faster between rebuild)

The code is easy to read and integrate, so feel free to feedback and questions

body:
I experienced similar problems in this Create React App project: https://github.com/lgk-bsw/msagl-react-with-problems
```
ERROR in ./node_modules/@msagl/renderer-svg/dist/index.js 1:0-44
Module not found: Error: Can't resolve './rendererSvg' in 'C:\projects-temp\msagl-react-with-problems\node_modules\@msagl\renderer-svg\dist'
Did you mean 'rendererSvg.js'?
BREAKING CHANGE: The request './rendererSvg' failed to resolve only because it was resolved as fully specified
(probably because the origin is strict EcmaScript Module, e. g. a module with javascript mimetype, a '*.mjs' file, or a '*.js' file where the package.json contains '"type": "module"').
The extension in the request is mandatory for it to be fully specified.
Add the extension to the request.

webpack compiled with 4 errors
```

body:
If the 'models.js' file does exist in the 'src' folder and is being correctly output to the 'dist' folder, then the issue may be with how the module is being imported and used in your application.

Here are a few things you could check:

Make sure that you're importing the module using the correct filename, without the file extension (e.g. import models from './dist/models')
Make sure that you're importing the module from the correct path. If the file is in the 'dist' folder, you'll need to import it using a relative path that starts with './' (e.g. import models from './dist/models')
Make sure that you're using the correct capitalization for the module name. JavaScript is case-sensitive, so 'models' and 'Models' are treated as different modules.
Check your application for any typographical errors in the import path.
If you are still facing the issue, you should check your bundle with webpack-bundle-analyzer, it will help you to see if your models.js is correctly included in your bundle or not.

Please let me know if this helps, or if you have any other questions.

Sorry for delay, but webpack tries to save memory (especial when cache enabled), so at some moment they do not have content and we store already processed files, i.e.
the first run - original content -> transform/generate -> cache results
the second run - get from cache and use it (and here you can have a problem)
the third run is the same as the second

So my qeustion why do not implement cache and avoid using `private` properties?

There is a potential place to cache https://github.com/yoriiis/svg-chunk-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.ts#L158, you can use logic from here - https://github.com/webpack-contrib/html-minimizer-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.js#L398

compilation.codeGenerationResults.get(NormalModule).sources.get('javascript').source()

body:
Sorry for delay 

> Ideally, I'd like to store the sprite in cache and only generate it when needed. How could i do that?

Yeah, for one file we use https://github.com/webpack-contrib/json-minimizer-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.js#L122, but for multiple fies you can use `cache.getLazyHashedEtag(source)` for each source (i.e. images) which you have and then merge etags using `cache.mergeEtags(etag1, etag2)`, then yse `cache.getItemCache(name, eTag)` (`name` is name of your ouput file, should not be absolute) to get cached item, if not found run your logic for generation and use `cacheItem.storePromise`

Just take a look at the `html-minimizer-webpack-plugin` plugin and implement the same logic only for multiple input images

Feel free to feedback if you need help

author: alexander-akait

body:
The loader exports the content of the SVG asset as a JSON string. Then, the plugin get the source of each SVG with the code below:

```js
JSON.parse(compilation.codeGenerationResults.get(normalModule).sources.get('javascript').source())
```

But, the sprite is composed by the plugin, it is the merge of severals SVG according to the entrypoint dependencies.

---

The following entries

```js
// entry-1.js
import 'svg-a.svg'
import 'svg-b.svg'
import 'svg-c.svg'
```

```js
// entry-2.js
import 'svg-a.svg'
import 'svg-d.svg'
import 'svg-e.svg'
```

Will produce

```html
<!-- entry-1.svg -->
<svg>
    <symbol id="svg-a"></symbol>
    <symbol id="svg-b"></symbol>
    <symbol id="svg-c"></symbol>
</svg>
```

```html
<!-- entry-2.svg -->
<svg>
    <symbol id="svg-a"></symbol>
    <symbol id="svg-d"></symbol>
    <symbol id="svg-e"></symbol>
</svg>
```

body:
> The first build found NormalModule dependencies, but the following not. It appears only when the cache is enabled.

That is expected because on the second run webpack doesn't run the loader, otherwise why we need cache? :smile: 

So the solution - calculate `eTag` in a place where you get sources and keep the hash of the previous build, so before run you will have `eTag = undefined`, after first run you will have `eTag = "ab12cd34"`, on the second run because `eTag` was not changed (because you don't have sources and nothing was changed) you just reemit cached `const cacheItem = cache.getItemCache(spriteName, eTag);` (sprite name you can get from options, and eTag from previous build)

Also you always have `hash` of build so you can undestand that nothing was changed, can you try to implement a basic cache logic (on the first run) and I can help you to implement a cache logic for the second/third/etc runs (if you stuch with it)

body:
Example of usage:
```
const eTag = cache.getLazyHashedEtag(source);
const cacheItem = cache.getItemCache(name, eTag);
```

`mergeEtags` and `getLazyHashedEtag` return the same `Etag` - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/types.d.ts#L614

So you can use any, but in your case you need to merge tags because you have multiple source objects

body:
@alexander-akait I'm adding some info about this problem (runs are done in production mode).

My idea was that if there is no cache available, it is the first run, so I'll generate all the files and update the cache.

On the second run:
- if the compilation hash is the same as the previous one, there is no change so I use the cache
- if the compilation hash is different, there are changes and I do the sprite generation and cache update process

But, the 2nd run generates a different hash while there is no change, the logic would be to do the sprite generation and cache update process, but the compilation does not return any modules, which seems normal to me since there were no changes.

1. Why does the hash change on the 2nd compilation? I don't see how to detect if I should use cache or not based on file changes (and hash). I don't reproduce this problem in development mode, nor with the cache disabled.
2. With the cache enabled, webpack loader seems not to fire when there are changes, why? It can generate inaccurate files.
author: yoriiis

body:
> But, the 2nd run generates a different hash while there is no change, the logic would be to do the sprite generation and cache update process, but the compilation does not return any modules, which seems normal to me since there were no changes.

Yeah, that is why we should use only eTag from sources... I need to look at your code (hope will find time soon), anyway without looking at code I have two solutions here:
1. Get changed files and undestand you have or not have changed files which used in sprite
2. Another way use modules as cache and use WeakMaps (to avoid using extra memory) to when module(s) will changed you will have other ETag and it run sprite compilation and emit a new asset

> With the cache enabled, webpack loader seems not to fire when there are changes, why? It can generate inaccurate files.

Yeah, because you have cached files, so you don't need to spend time to run code again

body:
I've found a solution.

it ends up that in my repository, we have a couple stray .jsx files (it's a typescript project), and our folder structure is in such a way:

shared
- index.ts (exporting everything below)
-- lots_of_component_folders
----> index.tsx

And the stray .jsx files were trying to import some compoments exported from shared/index.ts and that wasn't working, so after I pointed to the folder directly instead of to the /shared/ folder, it worked.

For example:
import { Component } from "shared"; <<<< BUG
import { Component } from "shared/ui/Component"; <<<< WORKS

Disabling FAST_REFRESH by creating a .env and putting FAST_REFRESH=false also worked, but I didn't want to have this disabled so I ended up fidnign this solution.

body:
Please don't use `raw-loader`, it is deprecated, please use https://webpack.js.org/guides/asset-modules/#replacing-inline-loader-syntax, i.e.

```js
{
  test: /\.txt$/i,
  type: "asset/source"
}
```

And use:
```js
import txtFile from "./someTxtFile.txt"
```

Or you can implement `?raw`:
```js
{
  resourceQuery: /raw/,
  type: 'asset/source',
}
```

and use:
```js
import txtFile from "./someTxtFile.txt?raw"
```

body:
Hello, sorry for delay, why you have `symlinks: false` with the package manager based on symlinks...

Please remove:
```
resolve: {
  extensions: [".js"],
  symlinks: false,
},
```

You don't need `extensions: [".js"],` globally too for resolver, because you will lost resolving cjs/mjs files. I recommend do not touch this at all and override it if you need on loader level (look our documentation), expect when you really need a new extension.

You can put `console.log("test");` in `./esm/vs/editor/editor.main.js` (in `monaco-editor` package), with `symlinks: false` and without and you will see that resolving doesn't work as expected. 

if you want to debug and see why it happens you can look at loader in the plugin and will see how code generated with and without option

body:
In this case, it can be problem, because webpack is thinking a package has the same content, there are some solutions:
1. https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cacheversion you can hash your `package.json` and use it in version, so any change will be invalid cache (not effective, but if you don't have it very often there is no problems)
2. Exclude your package from https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachemanagedpaths, somewhere I provide examples good reexps here, but I think it will not a problem to write them
3. You can add https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachebuilddependencies your package there, should work but I don't tests, maybe you still need to exclude it from https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachemanagedpaths

body:
I see, thank you.

There is one last approach I am thinking about: You know, in the end I am trying to inline some "compiled" JavaScript in a `<script />` tag via React and SSR.

And I am doing this as part of a Next.js application. 

So I wondering if this would work: A loader could add the requested file as an entry point its compiler. And at build time, instead of returning the file's contents, it would return code that would read the source code of the compiled code at runtime.

So something like this:

```js
// my-loader.js (mock code)

module.exports = function () {

  this._compiler.addEntry(this.resourcePath);

  return `module.exports = require("fs").readFileSync(/*output path for this file's entry*/);`;
}
```

Leaving aside questions like server vs. client side access to the file system (I have some ideas how to deal with that inside next js), it seem feasable to me that

* at build time, by dynamically adding an entry point, I would get the source code assets that I need
* at build time, I would generate code that will instruct the runtime to read that asset
* and at run time, during ssr, the generated entry point would be read from the file system and be used in my application
  (again I am assuming here that I have a workaround for dealing with the client, in short, next has a multicompiler, one for ssr one for the browser and I would generated different code for each one)


My issue with that approach is, that I cannot figure out how (or if) it is possible to dynamically add entries to the compiler from a loader
author: LukasBombach

body:
> My issue with that approach is, that I cannot figure out how (or if) it is possible to dynamically add entries to the compiler from a loader

Yeah, it was not desig for this, but you can try use this https://github.com/webpack/webpack-dev-server/blob/master/lib/Server.js#L676, don't forget to add this to dependecies, i.e. `this.addDependency(pathToFile);` for cache invalidation

body: I apologize for oversimplifying my previous example. In fact I have a library that can spawn multiple web workers, and I am trying to find a way to create a general webpack rule that will process all of the workers in my final application (which uses this library), without having to specify a separate build for each worker or know the entry path for each worker.
author: gretzkiy

body:
> without having to specify a separate build for each worker

I don't think it is possible because your library cosume worker files... Anyway maybe you can provide a reproducible example so I take a look and try to find the best solution
author: alexander-akait

body:
Sure, here it is. https://github.com/gretzkiy/webpack-url-assets-test

I've been able to achieve almost everything I wanted, but the final step is to set a custom output path for all workers, so that they can be placed in `dist/worker` instead of just `dist` (that's why I tried to use `asset/resource` with custom output path in my first example). 

Here I load the workers inside the library: https://github.com/gretzkiy/webpack-url-assets-test/blob/957b0a570b193752ce8fd51fbe4830d4b7a6490a/lib/src/index.ts#L6

The library is then used in the project: https://github.com/gretzkiy/webpack-url-assets-test/blob/957b0a570b193752ce8fd51fbe4830d4b7a6490a/project/src/index.ts#L3 

In the project's webpack configuration file, I have added the new `workerPublicPath` option: https://github.com/gretzkiy/webpack-url-assets-test/blob/957b0a570b193752ce8fd51fbe4830d4b7a6490a/project/webpack.config.js#L14

However, I'm having trouble figuring out how to place the processed worker files within a custom output path. I believe I can accomplish this using a webpack plugin, but I was wondering if you could suggest a better approach that uses native webpack tooling?
author: gretzkiy
alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait assisted me in troubleshooting this issue, and the outcome is as follows:

1. Currently, there is no simple method to modify the chunk name exclusively for the worker.
2. A somewhat unreliable approach exists (outlined in the linked issue below), but it is not considered dependable.
3. We have submitted a feature request to introduce a new option that can be used to designate a chunk filename solely for workers: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/17002.

author: alexander-akait

body:
> You can use dependecies and they can be written with any lines, sources maps are output as is, it is `source`, i.e. as they were written

Good point, I forget to consider that. But for my use case (creating artifact with reproducible content, regardless of OS) that does not seem to be an issue because the source maps of dependencies are downloaded during build, so their line endings are preserved (even if that happens to be `\r\n`) and therefore always consistent.

> To be honest, I don't understand the problem, most likely you have already tried to find the answer, but found nothing, because it makes no sense
> ...
> And what do you mean by source maps? sourceContent inside? The source map file itself?

To provide a specific example:
1. Download the content of https://github.com/webpack/webpack.js.org/tree/main/examples/getting-started
2. To simplify the example:
    1. Remove the `"lodash": "^4.17.21"` dependency from `package.json`
    2. Change `src/index.js` content to just:
       ```javascript
       function component() {
           const element = document.createElement("div");
           return element;
       }
       
       document.body.appendChild(component());
       ```
3. Add `devtool: 'source-map',` to `webpack.config.js`
4. Run `npm install`
5. Run `npm run build`
6. Inspect the content of `dist/main.js.map`

If you are working on Windows, cloned the repository with Git and have `core.autocrlf=true` (the default on Windows), or if you used in step 2.2 an editor which uses CRLF by default, then `src/index.js` and therefore also `dist/main.js.map` contains `\r\n` instead of just `\n`, e.g.:
```
... "sourcesContent":["function component() {\r\n  const element = document.createElement(\"div\");\r\n  return element;\r\n} ...
```

So, if you create an application and want the checksum of the `dist/main.js.map` file to be the same regardless on which machine you build the application, this might fail because on Windows the file might contain `\r\n`, whereas on Linux it will only contain `\n` and therefore have a different checksum.

In the context of this example, basically I want the source in `dist/main.js.map` to always use `\n`, regardless of whether `src/index.js` used `\n` or `\r\n`.

body:
Personally I currently think in general you should let users use whatever newline settings they want or what their code editors need, except for files which need specific line endings, e.g. LF for Linux `.sh` scripts. Being more familiar with compiled languages, I considered source code and final release artifacts two separate things, where ideally the release artifact should be reproducible. So from that perspective I would still expect that newline normalization is at least a configurable option.

Though I understand that not everyone might see it that way (maybe my view on this will change as well), and I know that supporting multiple types of newlines can complicate things, so I am fine with the `.gitattributes` solution for now since that seems to work reliably for me.

I have therefore marked this question as answered; thanks for taking the time to answer this!

This code is wrong https://github.com/MeganAlexia/webpack-nextjs-bug-repro/blob/main/webpack/plugin/test-plugin.js#L31:
- You should not use `compiler.hooks.emit` to emit assets, we have `compiler.hooks.compilation` and ` compilation.hooks.processAssets`
- You should not write assets manually using real fs, you need to emit them

Take a look at the simple JSON minimizer plugin https://github.com/webpack-contrib/json-minimizer-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.js:
- For you case you need https://github.com/webpack-contrib/json-minimizer-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.js#L199 to use another stage - `PROCESS_ASSETS_STAGE_ADDITIONAL` - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/Compilation.js#L5223
 - For loader you can use `emitFile` - https://webpack.js.org/api/loaders/#thisemitfile, if you need to emit something like assets
 - Don't forget to cache it to avoid extra execution (you can find an example of logic in `json-minimizer-webpack-plugin`)
 - To emit assets in a plugin you can use https://webpack.js.org/api/compilation-object/#emitasset
 
 Feel free to feedback

author: scameron

body:
> That is a really good idea. Do you know where the code for the `optimizePackageImports` implementation is kept? I couldn't find it in the Next.js repo.

Next.js's solution is somehow heavy.
- on Rust side integrated with swc, [`named_import_transform.rs`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/c0a7c842563e3722dea30b00a1ac4fd0a18c6cbf/crates/next-custom-transforms/src/transforms/named_import_transform.rs) transform the imports to a barrel-file-lib into `'__barrel_optimize__?names={}!=!{}'` format to keep track of which named imports are used, [`optimize_barrel.rs`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/c0a7c842563e3722dea30b00a1ac4fd0a18c6cbf/crates/next-custom-transforms/src/transforms/optimize_barrel.rs) process the barrel file and exports some meta informations
  - `__next_private_export_map__` describes the exports information of a barrel file
  - wildcard export is processed in `__barrel_optimize__?names=__PLACEHOLDER__!=!{src}` format
  - `__next_private_directive_list__` describes directives(`use client`, `use server`)
- on Webpack/Rspack side, [`next-barrel-loader`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/7453d200579512a6574f9c53edd716e5cc01615c/packages/next/src/build/webpack/loaders/next-barrel-loader.ts) calls transform(provided by Rust implementation) on the file, and does optimizations for the barrel file based on information Rust side provided.

e.g., One importer file A has an `import { foo, bar } from 'baz'` and 'baz' is a package using a barrel file as an entry file
1. swc transform it to `import { foo, bar } from '__barrel_optimize__?names=foo,bar!=!baz'`
2. a request with `__barrel_optimize__` hits `next-barrel-loader` loader
3. Loader parses the export map, resolves only the needed exports
4. For file A, loader returns the contents like `export { foo } from './foo'; export { bar } from './bar'`
5. Thus, webpack bundles only `node_modules/baz/dist/foo.js` and `node_modules/baz/dist/bar.js`

Notice: This only represents my personal opinion and may not be correct.

body: Barrel files are such an anti-pattern. The convenience is not worth the headache. Try to avoid them and instead be explicit about where your imports are coming from. Doing so also helps you to avoid cycles in your architecture.
author: morganney

body: How would you propose writing a modular architecture without barrel files? Typically, each module has a public api defined in a barrel file. This can be enforced through linters.
author: jmroon

body:
I guess I don't understand what problem you are trying to solve?

What could be more modular about an architecture than actually using [named exports](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules#exporting_module_features), [imports using the module specifier](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules#importing_features_into_your_script), [import maps](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules#importing_modules_using_import_maps) if you want to define specifiers differently, or [renaming imports and exports](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules#renaming_imports_and_exports) if you need to avoid name collisions of some kind?

> Typically, each module has a public api defined in a barrel file.

From my personal experience I'm advocating against that. I recognize [barrel files as an anti-pattern](https://www.google.com/search?q=barell+file+anti+pattern) for most projects. Most of the time they will:

* [Increase the size of your build](https://vercel.com/blog/how-we-optimized-package-imports-in-next-js)
* Cause [cyclic imports](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules#cyclic_imports)

Both of which you want to avoid.

If you want to expose a public API from a module, then use **named exports** (you can have as many as you like in a module) and only `export { somePublicFunctionality }` which can reference other identifiers and benefit from the closure over module scope.

> How would you propose writing a modular architecture without barrel files?

In short, use [ES module](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules) features.
author: morganney

body:
They cause cyclic imports when the modules are not obeying the "rules of modules" and they are not structured in a way to obey hierarchy of dependencies. That's just a sign that you have bad modules, not that barrels are bad. Instead barrels help you root them out.

Yes, existing tooling isn't very friendly to barrels. That's a disadvantage, but I wouldn't call barrels an anti pattern, which is something library/tooling authors keep parroting about because they get tired of people complaining about bundle sizes, slow bundling/compilation and such.
author: anilanar

body:
> If you want to expose a public API from a module, then use **named exports** (you can have as many as you like in a module) and only `export { somePublicFunctionality }` which can reference other identifiers and benefit from the closure over module scope.

That's already something barrel users do. What they mean by a public api is they split their larger module into smaller modules, most of which are internal and shouldn't be available to the outside. That intent is made clear with barrel files.

That's basically the same idea with npm packages (and package managers for most other langs follow the same pattern!). An npm package that does one thing typically reexport everything that should be public in their main exports and deep importing has long been considered an anti-pattern, until "oh no barrels make tooling inefficient what do we do now, let's declare it an anti-pattern".

if I run `snippet` in a browser environment, it would import `browser_entry.js`. If I run `snippet` in a Worker, it would be ideal if we can get `worker_entry.js`- because right now I believe it still selects `browser_entry.js`.

I could be wrong, so please correct me on this. But from what I gathered, the only way to get `worker_entry.js` is to explicitly set the `target` to `worker`... but since we know when an entry-point is being used in a worker (e.g. `new Worker(new URL(...))`), shouldn't we automatically set the `target` to `worker`?

body:
It looks like the issue stems from `WorkerPlugin` creating an async/codesplit chunk:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/27cf3e59f5f289dfc4d76b7a1df2edbc4e651589/lib/dependencies/WorkerPlugin.js#L336-L354

This will just use the same `target` as the rest of the compilation, unfortunately, since there's no way to override `target` for a specific part of the module graph.

I've also tried setting `resolve.byDependency.worker.conditionNames` in `webpack.config.js`, but that only works for resolving the initial module of the worker, rather than the entire subgraph that is used by the worker.

If I am not mistaken, I think the only way to fix this is to have `WorkerPlugin` create a child compiler to compile the worker (sub)graph with the `target` option set to `webworker`.

body:
I found the problem, but I don't understand the root cause.

In `OccurrenceModuleIdsPlugin` it applies a [sort to modulesInOccurrenceOrder](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/ids/OccurrenceModuleIdsPlugin.js#L135) but in many cases the `occursInInitialChunksMap` lookup returns `NaN` and since all comparisons with `NaN` are falsy, the sort becomes non-deterministic.

How does `occursInInitialChunksMap` end up with `NaN` values in it?  The value comes, in part, from [countOccursInEntry](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/ids/OccurrenceModuleIdsPlugin.js#L76) where sometimes this line returns `undefined`:

    initialChunkChunkMap.get(originModule);

The reason is that `originModule`, returned from `moduleGraph.getIncomingConnectionsByOriginModule` does not exist in the `initialChunkChunkMap`.  Looking a bit further back, it also doesn't exist in the `modulesInOccurrenceOrder` array.

The `modulesInOccurrenceOrder` array is populated in `IdHelpers.getUsedModuleIdsAndModules` and in there you can see that it only includes modules that are in [at least one chunk](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/ids/IdHelpers.js#L261).  My `NaN` modules, for some reason, are in zero chunks.

How can a module be in zero chunks when it is the origin module for incoming connections to other modules (which are in chunks)?
author: scameron

body: Sounds like a bug, I think we missed extra check somewhere, maybe you can isolate it in reproducible example (for future tests) and we will investigate deeply

I did notice that the zero chunk module seems to contain only imports and an export and not much else.  Does that ring any bells?  Maybe around inlining or some other kind of internal optimization?

body:
Everything works as expected:

> node-fetch has "sideEffects": false in its package.json, and

You have `import * as __WEBPACK_EXTERNAL_MODULE_node_fetch_e2fb16d6__ from "node-fetch";` on the top, because it is external and `sideEffects` will not work in such case
author: alexander-akait
alexander-akait

body: You need `externalsType` due this https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/16181 we will fix it in future
author: alexander-akait

body:
> Everything works as expected…

Not yet – not for me anyway. The [Webpack docs](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/examples/side-effects/README.md) have this to say about `sideEffects`:

> The "sideEffects": false flag in big-module's package.json indicates that the package's modules have no side effects (on evaluation) and only expose exports. This allows tools like webpack to optimize re-exports.

I don't think it's controversial to interpret that flag as an indicator that – absent a use – an import can be reaped. If `node-fetch` was not marked as `"sideEffects": false` and Webpack left this code alone:

```ts
require('node-fetch');
```

…that would be understandable! We can't know that the _module factory_ of `node-fetch` is side-effect free (eg. it _might_ overwrite `globalThis.fetch`) so we _have_ to leave it alone, to be safe.

That's not what's going on here. `node-fetch` is marked as side-effect free, it's not used anywhere in the body of the compiled `index.ts`, but despite all of that, Webpack doesn't reap it.

Why?
author: steveluscher
steveluscher

body:
> node-fetch is marked as side-effect free
> That's not what's going on here. node-fetch is marked as side-effect free, it's not used anywhere in the body of the compiled index.ts, but despite all of that, Webpack doesn't reap it.

It is not applied for externals, because we don't know what is `node-fetch`, it can be a package, it can be a variable in global scope, it can be anything in scope and can be loaded from `window`, `network` (using http/https) and etc, so we can't undestand it is `sideEffects` free, so it will not work and never worked




> That code exports a Promise for a function, rather than a function, so that will not work. Also, import("node-fetch") still appears in the built output, so it doesn't achieve the goal.

Try to run it again, maybe you missing something, all works perfectly locally, it remove `node-fetch`, but yes, it resturns promise, you can await it too when you need or using top level awaits and don't have it at all
author: alexander-akait

body:
> It is not applied for externals, because we don't know what is `node-fetch`…

I don't understand this. When you write `require('foo')` or `import X from 'foo'`, then `foo` is uncontroversially a module, right? Especially considering it has an entry in `package.json` under `dependencies`. The ESLint unused import linter, for instance, has no trouble identifying this require/import as unused. What is to stop Webpack from doing the same?
author: steveluscher

body:
> …you can await it too when you need…

I can't. This is a shim for the `fetch` API. I can't change it from sync to async at will, because downstream consumers won't be compatible unless its sync.
author: steveluscher

body: For ESM webpack by default move externals at top due https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/16181 (yes we want to fix it in future) and for externals we don't check `sideEffects`, that is why you have `node-fetch` at the top and it is not removed, the solution above, you need using async functions and `import(...)`, also you can rewrite it on top level awaits
author: alexander-akait

body: You seem to have indicated that this is a known problem, and fixable. I'm disappointed in your decision to have migrated this issue to a GitHub discussion. Now we can't track it as a feature request.
author: steveluscher

body: @steveluscher I'm afraid it's not entirely possible without rewriting the code to async (currently), because as I said above externals are not checking on `sideEffects`, so we can't just drop it, because it is unsafe, I reopen an another issue - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15486, because you are not the first who asked about it, so let's track it there, anyway PR welcome, it should be not hard to implement

body:
Many thanks. For reference, I'm using apache to serve the main application on app.com, and using amazon cloudfront to serve the react component and workers on cdn.com. If I understand correctly, you're suggesting setting up apache to proxy requests for the react component, so to the browser those resources appear to be served from app.com (same origin), but in reality the proxy is pulling them from cdn.com on the server side. Is that what you have in mind? If so, that approach seems counter to the main purpose of using the cdn, which is to offload / distribute traffic, be more responsive, and support a flexible deployment architecture.

Are there other options / suggestions for getting this to work?
author: dr-matt

body:
> If so, that approach seems counter to the main purpose of using the cdn, which is to offload / distribute traffic, be more responsive, and support a flexible deployment architecture.

I suggested this for use in development mode because it's easier to set up and works great, not for production

For production usage you need to setup right headers, of course you can create worker from blob, but it is not a good solution
author: alexander-akait

body:
I see, thank you. Fortunately in dev mode, everything works fine - there are no cross origin issues since all content is served from localhost, and workers can be created in the normal way without blobs.

I agree that creating a worker from a blob is not the ideal solution. It seems hacky and causes other problems downstream, like the one described here. But I haven't been able to find a working solution for production; even with CORS headers and CSP `worker-src` header, I still see the original `failed to construct Worker` error mentioned above. My understanding from reading other discussions was that using blobs is the only way around this, but if there's a better method, I'd much prefer that.
author: dr-matt

body: In theory you can try to use `importScripts`, but if your worker is in ESM, it will not work
author: alexander-akait

body:
Can you use absolute publicPath or do you need relative one?
The issue is when you try to import another script using relative path. It's because blob worker has `blob://...` origin. You can use absolute paths or overwrite `importScript()` function like here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/14648#discussioncomment-2559969
author: piotr-oles

body:
@piotr-oles I do need relative `publicPath` as this component is used in different directory locations for different apps. And you're exactly right about the new `blob://` origin. 

Thank you for the link on replacing `importScripts`. That code didn't work for me as written when I tried it a while back, but I was eventually able to make it work with some even more hacky modifications. Unfortunately it leads to other downstream problems, as my particular worker also needs (and fails) to load emscripten-compiled webassembly modules included in the webpack bundle. I will try continuing down that path, but it doesn't feel like a stable long-term solution - is there really no cleaner way to serve components like this from a different host?
author: dr-matt

body:
Update: it looks like the webassembly module load is failing because the underlying javascript glue is using `fetch` to get the wasm resource, and it's trying to fetch from origin `blob:...` just like `importScripts` does. So if I apply the same function replacement trick to redefine `self.fetch`, I can get everything to load.

A remaining issue is that the component is typically served from a subdirectory under the web root, and that subdirectory is omitted from the url in some (but not all)  `importScripts` and `fetch` calls. I don't know yet how to resolve this.

body:
> Another question is about cache. Before it was deprecated, it was set to true by default. Does it still behave the same?

Webpack has built-in cache https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachetype, if you set `type: 'filesystem',`, you will cache not only terser output, but all webpack build (try it and the next build will be faster), note - when you use `filesystem` memory cache will be used too (it is like layers)

> Regarding sourceMap I understand that it now considers Webpack's devtool option instead. BUT what if for Webpack and terser-webpack-plugin we need different source map settings? How do I set sourceMap to false for terser-webpack-plugin when I have devtool set to something else?

What is `devtool` value for production? It was removed because you can break source maps, that is why terser plugin look at the `devtool` option

body:
> But if terser doesn't know then how does it remove the code of the component (the TextWithIcon, see below)? The code there also doesn't have /*@__PURE__*/ annotation?

Terser is just a minifier, it doesn't know nothing about `sideEffects: false`, because it is on the webpack level

there is the `pure_funcs` option in terser

> pure_funcs (default: null) -- You can pass an array of names and Terser will assume that those functions do not produce side effects. DANGER: will not check if the name is redefined in scope. An example case here, for instance var q = Math.floor(a/b). If variable q is not used elsewhere, Terser will drop it, but will still keep the Math.floor(a/b), not knowing what it does. You can pass pure_funcs: [ 'Math.floor' ] to let it know that this function won't produce any side effect, in which case the whole statement would get discarded. The current implementation adds some overhead (compression will be slower).


Anyway give me a time, I will look deeply

body:
@alexander-akait I made some experiments with terser and I think I understand now the behavior I get.
From what I've seen, terser knows to analyze js expressions until certain level of complexity. If it succeeds to analyze an expression and deduce that for sure it has no other code imports it and that it has no side effects, then it removes it (and the variable that holds it). But when the expression is too complex and terser can't deduce that there are no side effects, it doesn't remove it.

I may be wrong, but that is I think what happend with the TextIconStyle variable I have in the code, which holds a rather complex expression.

If you intend to look into it sometime, I would love to know your conclusions.
Anyway, thanks for responding me and for the tips you gave me along the way. I learned a lot and I really appriciate it :)
author: yishayweb

body:
I saw only now the comment about you testing the code.
You are right, `TextWithIcon` is not in the chunks. It is removed. But `TextIconStyle` is remained although it is unused

I would love to hear your thoughts on the previous comment I made about expressions complexity. Does it sound right to you?
author: yishayweb

body:
I read more into details [this part of tree shaking docs](https://webpack.js.org/guides/tree-shaking/#clarifying-tree-shaking-and-sideeffects) and I think it's validating what I wrote [in the previous comment](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/17073#discussioncomment-5758977)
In the docs it says that terser has difficulties determining if code like this `withAppProvider()(Button)` (react HOC) has side effects or not.
From the docs:
`Terser actually tries to figure it out, but it doesn't know for sure in many cases`

This HOC case from the docs is similar to the `TextIconStyle` I asked about because it holds an expression that is something like this `styled$1(someArg)(someOtherArg)`. It has the same structure like the HOC case in the docs.

Thanks again @alexander-akait for helping me out with this :)
Should I mark this discussion as 'Answered'?

body:
I'm using [electron-react-boilerplate](https://github.com/electron-react-boilerplate/electron-react-boilerplate/blob/main/.erb/configs/webpack.config.renderer.dev.ts), minus the web stuff there. 

Note that this websocket issue didn't occur in webpack 4, only happened after upgrading to webpack 5, webpack cli, dev-server and [react refresh plugin](https://github.com/pmmmwh/react-refresh-webpack-plugin) to latest. 

I think it's related to react refresh plugin not respecting the websocketUrl values, since I have a non-standard url. http:localhost:port/dist, it gives a connection error since it's trying to connect to ws://localhost/ws. 

The package author said they'd find time to do an update, but who knows when that is. Hence the best solution for now would be straight up ignoring the websocket error message that comes from webpack-dev-server and hot reloading

Hi.

Reproduction is ready.
I think the problem is that `extended-resolver` does not take in account `node --preserve-symlinks` option and unable to resolve dependencies.
Please, help me to solve this.

### How to reproduce

```shell
# clone repo
git clone https://github.com/zdm/webpack-build-dependencies-issue-17169

cd webpack-build-dependencies-issue-17169
cd main

# this command:
# - installs dependencies
# - links "package-a" and "package-b" to the "main/node_modules"
npm i

# this command runs:
# node --preserve-symlinks node_modules/webpack/bin/webpack.js
npm run build
```

Build is OK, but cache is not used due to the following errors:

```txt

author: zdm

body: Seems, that `extended-resolver` is configured to resolve dependencies for sub-packages starting from the sub-package root dir, but the correct way is to resolve them starting from the main module root dir.
author: zdm

body: I want to say it is a limitation, we never support `--preserve-symlinks`, also you packages use each other but do not declare it in their dev dependencies, technically it is possible to implement the possibility of configuring internal revolvers, but I'm not sure it will be worth the effort (very exotic case). I would recommend using - https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v8/using-npm/workspaces, this is a more supported and official way to work with multiple packages in one repository.
author: alexander-akait

body:
This is not so rare case, this is very helpful when you for example need to work both on the end-user application and the shared web components libraries.

Interesting, that deps resolver failed only for build dependencies, but works for the same dependencies in the main build process.
Maybe fix can be simple?
author: zdm

body:
> This is not so rare case, this is very helpful when you for example need to work both on the end-user application and the shared web components libraries.

Can you show me other projects? I still recommend to use https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v8/using-npm/workspaces

Your dependency tree is not quite correctly built, which can lead to extremely difficult and complex bugs.

https://nodejs.org/api/cli.html#--preserve-symlinks

From docs:

> Note, however, that using --preserve-symlinks can have other side effects. Specifically, symbolically linked native modules can fail to load if those are linked from more than one location in the dependency tree (Node.js would see those as two separate modules and would attempt to load the module multiple times, causing an exception to be thrown).

> Interesting, that deps resolver failed only for build dependencies, but works for the same dependencies in the main build process.
Maybe fix can be simple?

There is no concept of fix here, we just follow default resolution node.js logic

 > technically it is possible to implement the possibility of configuring internal revolvers, but I'm not sure it will be worth the effort (very exotic case)
 
 Here is place https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/FileSystemInfo.js#L1513

We can do it, I doubt it only because all the time using webpack you are the first and only one who has encountered this...

>  technically it is possible to implement the possibility of configuring internal revolvers, but I'm not sure it will be worth the effort (very exotic case)

I honestly can't understand what the problem is with using dependencies using `package.json` (`dependencies` and `devDependencies`), you don't specify dependencies directly, so they don't appear in `node_modules` and as i said earlier such behavior can lead to unpredictable results, for example duplicated packets can be considered as completely different, which will make your `bundle` bigger. 

I want to help you, I want to say that's such behavior is extremely undesirable and you could potentially get into serious trouble - your dependency tree is broken. The problem will be especially evident with transitive dependencies. And I don’t understand why you ignore the direct words from the author of the project that this approach is dangerous. 

As well as direct words from the developer of the node:

> Note, however, that using --preserve-symlinks can have other side effects. Specifically, symbolically linked native modules can fail to load if those are linked from more than one location in the dependency tree (Node.js would see those as two separate modules and would attempt to load the module multiple times, causing an exception to be thrown).

body:
> How can I trace what is in these cache files to understand why they're so large?

It's quite a challenge, but you can try https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cacheprofile

> What can I do to optimize the size of the cache, any tips?

Compress it https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachecompression

> Why does the cache grow on sequential builds (index.pack.old files suddenly exist as well as 2.pack and 3.pack)? How can I ensure only the latest cache data is being kept on disk?

You can set https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachemaxage, but I don't think it really helps to solve the problem (and I saw you already tried it)

What is a project size? With images/video/etc? But I agree, this is a big size (but I saw more :smile: ), do you have something experimental for next.js, if yes, can you try to disable it and check again? For example next.js generates a images (with different sizes) on the bundling step, so it will be cached by webpack, also you can just open file using any text editor and found indexes and content of text files
author: alexander-akait

body:
> > What can I do to optimize the size of the cache, any tips?
> 
> Compress it https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachecompression

The docs linked there say that gzip compression is used by default in production mode, so I assume I'm already benefitting from that?

> > Why does the cache grow on sequential builds (index.pack.old files suddenly exist as well as 2.pack and 3.pack)? How can I ensure only the latest cache data is being kept on disk?
> 
> You can set https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachemaxage, but I don't think it really helps to solve the problem (and I saw you already tried it)
> 
> What is a project size? With images/video/etc? But I agree, this is a big size (but I saw more 😄 ), do you have something experimental for next.js, if yes, can you try to disable it and check again? For example next.js generates a images (with different sizes) on the bundling step, so it will be cached by webpack, also you can just open file using any text editor and found indexes and content of text files

Yeah, we tried a max age of just 5 minutes, but even still the output seems to grow. A max age of 0 threw some internal webpack erros, but ideally I'd like to only ever persist what has just been used for the build.

https://github.com/alveusgg/alveusgg/pull/268#issuecomment-1545049469 contains `du -sh` outputs for the build -- looking at the fresh build under the `Testing sequential builds` heading, the entire output from Next.js for our client + server comes to 270MB (`.next/server` + `.next/static`), yet the Webpack cache (`.next/cache/webpack`) is a whopping 583MB.

We are not using any experiment Next.js features beyond scroll restoration, which is just a react-router feature, nothing that'd impact the bundle size.

body:

body: When you set `experiments.outputModule`, you need specify a type of your external, by default it will use `import` so you need to use `data:`, in commonjs you can have `const foo = require("my-external")`, so webpack replace it on `const foo = {polyfill(){}};`, but you can't use it in ESM, even more we strongly recommend to use path to module, not inline code, because it is very limited and works only in some cases, for example using `externalsType` (https://webpack.js.org/configuration/externals/#externalstype) with other values will be broken too (not for all, but for many)

body:
You can try `plugins: [new webpack.webworker.WebWorkerTemplatePlugin()]`, but I don't think it is what you want.

But I have some questions:
- Why you want to use classic script inside ESM?
- Do you have `import ... from ...` inside workers?

Because if you have we can't load ESM inside classic Worker, otherwise we need duplicate ESM and non ESM chunks, I don't think you want to do it.

The `workerChunkFormat` and `chunkFormat` may not be compatible, so as you can see there is no such option.

Alternative solution - multi compiler module, compile worker separately and them use it inside the main build
author: alexander-akait

body:
> You can try `plugins: [new webpack.webworker.WebWorkerTemplatePlugin()]`, but I don't think it is what you want.

It's unclear to me how to use this properly so I'd have to experiment.  Would I just specify `options.chunkFormat = "array-push"`?  Do I need to repeat all other output options?

> But I have some questions:
> 
>     * Why you want to use classic script inside ESM?

As stated in my OP, because Firefox doesn't support ESM workers without turning on a config flag, and `comlink` promises seem to just hang.


>     * Do you have `import ... from ...` inside workers?

Yes, but mainly just for `comlink`.  They are relatively tiny compared to the whole app.



> Because if you have we can't load ESM inside classic Worker, otherwise we need duplicate ESM and non ESM chunks, I don't think you want to do it.


That's exactly what I'd want.  The penalty for duplicating a small vendor package would be far over-shadowed by the speed gained by using ESM and native `import()` for the rest of the app.

> Alternative solution - multi compiler module, compile worker separately and them use it inside the main build

I considered that, but it's an awful lot of build script changes for something I would hope Webpack could handle.
author: steverep

body:
> It's unclear to me how to use this properly so I'd have to experiment. Would I just specify options.chunkFormat = "array-push"? Do I need to repeat all other output options?

Just add this to your configuration and you don't need to setup nothing extra, but code will not be in ESM format, that is why I think you don't want it

> That's exactly what I'd want. The penalty for duplicating a small vendor package would be far over-shadowed by the speed gained by using ESM and native import() for the rest of the app.

I am afraid webpack can't do it, because it was originally thought that such behavior is undesirable. Even adding `workerChunkFormat` will not solve it...

> I considered that, but it's an awful lot of build script changes for something I would hope Webpack could handle.

You don't need extra scripts, just set [`dependencies`](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/other-options/#dependencies) and webpack will run it before the main build.

Alternative solution - using babel/swc/etc code inliner, also you can inject code using `DefinePlugin`/`BannerPlugin` (they are hacks but will work and don't think we will break it, just non standard usage).
author: alexander-akait

body:
> Just add this to your configuration and you don't need to setup nothing extra, but code will not be in ESM format, that is why I think you don't want it


Without any options object, it does nothing to the workers and instead causes all `inport()` loading code to be replaced with `importScripts` i.e. for the entry chunks which of course doesn't make sense).  Specifying `module: false` or `chunkFormat: "array-push"` in the plugin options does nothing.

> I am afraid webpack can't do it, because it was originally thought that such behavior is undesirable. Even adding `workerChunkFormat` will not solve it...

😞 

> You don't need extra scripts, just set [`dependencies`](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/other-options/#dependencies) and webpack will run it before the main build.

Just so I know we're on the same page, you're talking about telling Webpack to ignore `new Worker()` (not sure how?), and then replacing with the output worker from the dependent config?

alexander-akait

body:
> and then in code use `new Worker(new URL("../worker-dir/worker-main.js", import.meta.url))`

Won't the app config just then just re-bundle the worker and replace the URL?

> Also there is a theoretical solution with `layer`, but it will require to use an extra entry, not `new Worker(...)` syntax and more options - https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#entry-descriptor

Yeah, I considered that as it looks like the only way to specify alternative chunk format in the same build but seems like too much of a pain.

The only other hack I was thinking might work would be to add a `splitChunks` rule to make sure the workers never have any chunks to load, and then go back and strip `{ type: "module" }` from the `new Worker()` call.
author: steverep

body:
> Won't the app config just then just re-bundle the worker and replace the URL?

Will but you can setup `noParse` to avoid it, but it is not a problem, somtimes you can have different babel/swc configurations for libraries/applications, so you can setup it based on what you want

> The only other hack I was thinking might work would be to add a splitChunks rule to make sure the workers never have any chunks to load, and then go back and strip { type: "module" } from the new Worker() call.

It might work, but that's right, it's a hack, so be careful when using it

I still recommend using multicompiler mode, it is not hard and more control and you don't need to modify commands and scripts, webpack will run everything under the hood

body:
Thanks but I figured out a much simpler way to do it:

```js
      splitChunks: {
        // Disable splitting for web workers
        chunks: (chunk) =>
          !chunk.canBeInitial() && // same as "async" (default)
          !/(sort_filter|markdown)_worker/.test(chunk.name),
      },
```

Not sure why I didn't try that first.  Still don't understand why the cache group didn't work, but this is better. 🤷🏻‍♂️ 

On another note, I'll be filing an issue soon because it appears to me that web workers simply do not work with ESM output if they have any external chunks to load.  What I thought originally was `comlink` occurs with any external chunk - the runtime never gets past the `__webpack_require__` of those chunks.
author: steverep

body:
> On another note, I'll be filing an issue soon because it appears to me that web workers simply do not work with ESM output if they have any external chunks to load. What I thought originally was comlink occurs with any external chunk - the runtime never gets past the __webpack_require__ of those chunks.

For `__webpack_require__` we fixed a bug recently, it doesn't generate valid ESM output if you have outputModule, please update to the latest version
author: alexander-akait

body:
So I just tested 5.86 with the bug fix, and workers still have the same issue.

I was scanning the code and noticed that with `importScripts` loading, the external chunks are included as a startup dependency, and there's a bunch of extra runtime code.  Looks like ESM loading is the only method that doesn't include the startup dependency plugin.  Probably not the problem at all, but figured I'd mention it in case it were just that simple.

body: Sorry, you can't have the same module ids in your case (you have two independent builds, they don't guarantee that you will always have the same IDs between builds, even more plugins and loader can inject more modules and even change module IDs), and I want to say it never works for webpack v4 (if you have id optimizations, so it was a luck if it works without any problems), you can't rely on ID(s) at all, I can't provide a valid setup here, but `isomorphic-style-loader` should calculate module ID based on content in the loader, so for the same content you will have the same ID, technically you can calculate ID(s) on the client side too, but it is bad for perfomance, I really don't know why `isomorphic-style-loader` rely in such things.

body:
Picking the thread apart a bit more I think the actual hashing is done in https://github.com/webpack/loader-utils/blob/0c98405f9ab36b26b2f47843c50023199c440a6d/lib/getHashDigest.js#L63 .

The buffer tells me what is the source string, Then it is hashed (which I don't understand) and then base64 encoded.

By following the logic this should produce the same result :

```php
$hash = hash('md4', 'src/app.module.cssslideshow', true);
$base64 = base64_encode($hash);
```
But it doesn't which means my logic is faulty :smile:

Reading that it was base64 encoded made me think it would be possible and not that hard, so I appreciate your input. 

If it is a waste of time I should either try and fix the bug, switch to postcss and use a plugin or start my own webpack config. Ironically I didn't do some of these things to save time :)

body:
Thanks for weighing in, I appreciate it. I my focus would be on the bug fix for sure.

I was looking at the logic which is used to generate the scoped classes and it didn't look too bad. Once you have the string (made up of filename, path and class) it appears to get an md4 hash to hash the string and base64 encode the outcome of that.

I am new to the node crypto library and I couldn't get it to print out the hash that is generated to test against the PHP version I made. Is the crypto md4 hash hard to reproduce because the hash is generated based on the environment?
author: JoshuaCrewe

body:
It is helpful to type out how I understand it so that I can see faulty logic or spark the learning process.

In `loader-utils` there is the function `getHashDigest` which is where the magic happens. We can follow the logic so far : 
```
  console.log(`the buffer is ${buffer} `); // src/file/class
  console.log(`the HashType is ${hashType} `); // md4
  console.log(`the digestType is ${digestType} `); // base64
  console.log(`the maxLength is ${maxLength} `); // 9999
```
But then it gets to the hashing part : 
```
hash = require("crypto").createHash(hashType);
...
hash.update(buffer);
...
return hash.digest(digestType || "hex").substr(0, maxLength);
``` 

I am unfamiliar with the hashing object which requires me reading the documentation closer, but the above three commands result in the scoped class name. It appears to : 
1. Create a hash using md4
2. use that hash object to transform the string
3. base64 encode this to get the scoped class name

I thought the equivalent in PHP would have been : 
```
$hash = hash('md4', 'src/app.module.cssslideshow', true);
$base64 = base64_encode($hash);
```
But the end result is different. The only variable is the hash that node generates, but if this can be replicated in PHP it should be OK. The question becomes how to translate the md4 hash in PHP then.

![2023-06-09_15-15](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/12238901/cfa7e5b3-269c-4b55-ba12-0eedb8988168)
author: JoshuaCrewe

body: There is a full logic https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-loader/blob/master/src/utils.js#L327, also you miss this one https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-loader/blob/master/src/utils.js#L351, we use unprintable character between local and module
author: alexander-akait

body: Ooo the unprintable character could be the thing which is trowing it off. I will try and find a way to make sure that is included in the process 🤞
author: JoshuaCrewe

body: Yep. That is the missing piece of this puzzle. There is the unprintable character which I missed when doing my rudimentary debugging. This means that the difference I was getting in my results were because the source string being hashed was different.

body:
You don't need `outputPath: "../fonts/"`, it was not design for such usage, set `filename: 'fonts/[hash][ext]'"` or `filename: 'images/[hash][ext]',` instead `outputPath`, avoid using `..` in filename at all, for JS you can use `filename: "javascript/scripts.js",`


For css:
```
new MiniCssExtractPlugin({
            filename: "css/[name].css",
            chunkFilename: "css/[id].css",
            ignoreOrder: false,
}),
```

Yes, but I don't reccommend, because you can have file overlapping and it will create long URLs in files so you will have bigger files.

> I have such meta tag, I want to webpack generate full path for this image, [www.link.com/images/avatar.png](http://www.link.com/images/avatar.png) . How should I do this?

Webpack generates relative URLs by default, if you need absolute URLs, you need to use full URL to your website in the `publicPath` option
