body:
I know this discussion has moved towards alternatives but to me it seems the underlying cause for this error is being ignored.

In the following code, https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/FileSystemInfo.js#L3029 the code is passing through a null error and result when the upstream filesystem error is ENOENT. Instead of handling this gracefully and providing details of the file in question, the subsequent call stack in _readContextTimestampAndHash.finalize produces an empty object `{}` this has the flow on effect of pushing a null value onto the hashes array in _resolveContextTsh.

The end result is a cryptic error in a unrelated subsystem rather than a nice clear error that a file wasn't found.

Thoughts?
author: lynkz-matt-psaltis

body:
Further debugging I found (for my use case at least) that this is coming from a pnpm based symlink in the node_modules folder within a monorepo turbo project. The storybook config was importing using `/packages/ui/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|ts|tsx)` which causes the symlink path to be concatenated with the symlink target so you end up with a path that looks like:

`C:\project\packages\ui\node_modules\eslint-config-customC:\project\packages\eslint-config-custom`
author: lynkz-matt-psaltis

body:
Just wanted to share my insights here for anyone else who runs into this. For me the issue was the in-memory caching. I had a React application, written in TypeScript, that would fail to recompile when running in the dev server if I didn't save the application entry point file each time I made a change. This was on macOS, mind, but the solution was to set `cache: false` in my development Webpack configuration.

You can browse the exact setup of my project and replicate the issue [at this commit](https://github.com/glektarssza/silent-gear-calculator/commit/d8870a0e18692b2dc5f7e9447f1f441b38eb0012).

Hope this helps anyone else who runs into this!
author: glektarssza

body: The issue also shows up when there's a problem with reading fileystem information for hashes. In my case it happens with a massively parallelised CI pipeline where `webpack` ended up scanning files it should not have scanned, causing it to fail as the files were being changed at the same time.
author: ConcernedHobbit

I encountered the same problem, which is very strange, but I didn't use `storybook`, and I turned off `cache`.

I used basic `webpack` and wrote a custom plugin that inserts additional entries during the compilation process.
If the entries are predefined instead of added later, the problem will not occur.

body:
Yeah, you have:
```
let dirName = (0,path__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.dirname)((0,url__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_1__.fileURLToPath)("file:///home/akait/IdeaProjects/test-extension/node_modules/lmdb/native.js")).replace(/dist$/, '');
```

Original code:
```
let dirName = dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)).replace(/dist$/, '');
```

But commonjs doesn't support `import.meta.url` (you know), so webpack just replace it on the static value

But good news - `lmdb` has CommonJS:
```
 "exports": {
    ".": {
      "types": {
        "require": "./index.d.cts",
        "import": "./index.d.ts"
      },
      "node": {
        "require": "./dist/index.cjs",
        "import": "./node-index.js"
      },
      "default": "./index.js"
    }
  },
```

But you have `module: "esnext"`, so `typescript` generates `import`s and `webpack` gets `import`, so just use:
```
 resolve: {
  alias: {
    'lmdb': path.resolve(__dirname, 'node_modules/lmdb/dist/index.cjs'),
  },
  modules: ['node_modules'],
  extensions: ['.ts', '.js', '.json'],
  mainFields: ["main", "module"]
},
```

But my recommendation is do not use `module: "esnext"` and `mainFields: ["main", "module"]` (i.e. do not add `module`) when your output is commonjs, because you can faced with such limitations.

When I fixed it in your repo I get:
```
ERROR in ./node_modules/lmdb/dist/index.cjs 2610:41-72
Module not found: Error: Can't resolve 'cbor-x' in '/home/akait/IdeaProjects/test-extension/node_modules/lmdb/dist'
 @ ./extension.ts 1:0-28 4:13-17
```

Installed it I got another one:
```
/home/akait/IdeaProjects/test-extension/out/extension.js:751
  var platformPackage = (packageName[0] == '@' ? '' : '@' + packageName + '/') + packageName + '-' + platform + '-' + arch
                                    ^

TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading '0')
    at load.path (/home/akait/IdeaProjects/test-extension/out/extension.js:751:37)
    at Object.load [as default] (/home/akait/IdeaProjects/test-extension/out/extension.js:720:25)
    at ./node_modules/lmdb/dist/index.cjs (/home/akait/IdeaProjects/test-extension/out/extension.js:3597:47)
    at __webpack_require__ (/home/akait/IdeaProjects/test-extension/out/extension.js:10301:41)
```

Because of (in `./node_modules/node-gyp-build-optional-packages/index.js`):
```
packageName = require(path.join(dir, 'package.json')).name
```

Maybe you don't want to compile this module or you want to exclude it or something else, I don't know the context, or maybe it's even expected behavior, if you provide more context then I can advise a solution.

body: I started making a repository and probably figured it out myself. It was  that I ran the compilation of the server and client bundles in the same process, and this resulted in a build build time that was higher than the starting volume build. I launched first `compiler.watch(server.config)` and then launched `new WebpackDevServer(client.config.devServer)`, but then I changed compiler.watch to compiler.run and everything worked as it should. perhaps they cannot work in parallel, and the second build can only be built after the first

By doing so, it helps catch potential type-related errors during development, provides better auto-completion and type inference in code editors, and improves overall code quality.

Here are the summarized changes for the update from v5.88.0 to v5.88.1:

Significantly improved TypeScript coverage.
Refactored codebase to enhance type annotations and declarations.
Added more type definitions to increase type safety and accuracy.
These changes are aimed at improving the development experience and ensuring better code quality within the Library Plugins project.

body: It's a implement of webpack to translate cjs esm module to webpack runtime. The primary reason for this transformation is that the <script> tag in HTML does not provide built-in support for module loading without `type:module`. When a JavaScript file is included using a <script> tag, it is treated as a global script, and the code within it is executed immediately in the global scope. If you interested in it, you can implement mini webpack to learn more.

body: You seem to be generating the hash as a hexstring and then converting that to bytes. Surely you mean to generate the hash as bytes from the onset?
author: ErikLindroos

body:
Right now im simply trying to understand and mimic the internal way Webpack is doing this. The goal is to have a better understanding of how Webpack generates this so i can create a custom Hasher to match our ASP.NET MVC Bundling Urls that use cache-busting by adding a querystring param based on a hash of the file it sees when creating the bundle.

But i need a better understand of how Webpack is doing the hashing before i can even try to create a custom one. So i figured if i can get a Powershell version to match Webpack then i would have a better understanding of how its doing that to create my custom hasher.

MVC Basically does the following
1. Read the file to string
2. Converts string to bytes using `Encoding.Unicode.GetBytes()`
3. Hashes that to `SHA256`
4. To Base64 with URL support

author: scameron

body:
Actually, the deprecated `AggressiveSplittingPlugin` seems to do this.  It only touches chunks that are bigger than `maxSize` and doesn't touch anything else.

The problem is that both  `SplitChunkPlugin` and `AggressiveSplittingPlugin` tap the same `optimizeChunks` hook and specify the same stage.  Since splitChunks is created internally, it always seems to run after a plugin I declare in my config.  And I definitely don't want `AggressiveSplittingPlugin` to run before splitChunks or it will really mess up the chunk splitting optimizations.

As an experiment, I changed the `AggressiveSplittingPlugin` tap to pass in a stage of `STAGE_ADVANCED + 1`, which makes it run after splitChunks is done.  This way, it only trims down the very largest chunks and doesn't touch anything else.

This seems to work well for my case, but it means using a deprecated plugin.  Would you be open to making this plugin work again for cases like mine?  The other option is to change the behavior of `maxSize` in splitChunks but I wouldn't even know where to begin with that...

What do you think?
author: scameron

body: Sorry for delay, is it still valid? We can implement `maxSizeStrict` (maybe better name), I don't think we really need `AggressiveSplittingPlugin`, anyway you can always implement an own plugin (just copy code from `AggressiveSplittingPlugin`) and do some magic here (what you want)
author: alexander-akait

body: Thanks for the reply.  In the end, that's what I ended up doing.  Our problem was happening in one particular case, so I implemented a small plugin inspired by a bit of the code from the built-in splitting plugins.  It seems to do the trick.  I set the `stage` to `STAGE_ADVANCED + 1` so that it runs after `SplitChunksPlugin`.

body:
> In my JSX (Foo.jsx): > `
` > compiles to: > `
` Unless I am missing something, `css-loader` does not handle the JSX at all. Its responsibility is limited to only load CSS files; and if the `modules` options is enabled, then the CSS classes in the output are renamed, and the `import` statement returns an object mapping original class names to the transformed ones. CSS loader does not know how you use this class name mapping after it has been imported into your javascript file. Personally, I have used your option 2; but also, tried to treat components styled with CSS modules as if they were proper web components with shadow DOM, and expose the properties that are allowed to be styled via CSS variables. I think option 3 breaks the very purpose of CSS modules. If you choose option 3, you could just as well have used a naming convention like BEM, which makes class name collisions unlikely, but does not give any guarantees.

body: i have provide a cloud ide. In the repo, i add a custom plugin to add a entry which will not be splitChunk. This case may usually appear in Micro-Frontend mode. You can imagine a Master App and a Child App, if Child App split chunk and some chunk are built into html as script tag. In this case if Master App load Child App's js file, some chunks will not be loaded forever, because it is build into html template. So Child App can not work. In the repo, main entry and second entry have its own hmr runtime, and the second entry is loaded after main entry, so the global hmr function will be override by second entry. Step: npm start -> refresh page -> modify second entry -> hmr success npm start -> refresh page -> modify main entry -> hmr fail https://stackblitz.com/edit/webpack-webpack-js-org-q8kgen?file=webpack.config.js,public%2Fsecond-entry.js,src%2Findex.js,public%2Findex.html,public%2Fadd-entry-plugin.js&terminal=build,start

author: nyqykk
alexander-akait

body:
Yes, It is worked when this an independent application. if we set runtimeChunk as 'single', main entry and the second entry will use the same runtime.js as there runtimeChunk. It perform that both two entry call __webpack_require__.o firstly to ensure runtimeChunk are loaded and then call __webpack_require__ to get the entry exports.

But i mean in Micro Frontend mode, this is a child application, and main.js and the second entry are the entry of the child application. If runtimeChunk are split into runtime.js file, when we load main.js or the second entry js file, runtime.js will not be loaded and then the application can not start up because both two entry miss some runtime chunk. 
This perform that both two entry also call __webpack_require__.o to ensure runtimeChunk are loaded, but it will not loaded forever because we split it into other runtime.js file, so the application can not work.

> Tell the compiler reference to invalidate its cache for the changed file(s)

It can be done, but it's not easy, why do you need? I feel you are doing something wrong
author: alexander-akait

body:
We have our own build systems for individual packages, performant file watcher which spans across projects and apps, and coordinating system to tell when each thing should do what. 

One example flow could be:
- File changes in a package
- Another file changes in a different package
- Our existing file watcher triggers (watching things cross packages / apps / projects)
- Our system sees that there is a dependency relationship between the packages, so it builds them in the correct order
   - For some older packages, post-processing can occur on the `dist` file content
- Our system sees everything has settled, knows that the packages are related to X applications, so it goes to safely trigger a rebuild on those applications.
 
Trying to fit Webpack 5 in for application builds without having to fully redo our existing processes.

Originally we were using Rollup which has a cache and rebuild mechanism. After a build you get a simple cache which you can quickly find a file/module by absolute path and then remove it. Triggering a build after that allows it to reuse the cache for everything else, but read from disk and re-process anything missing from cache.

Unfortunately Rollup is rather slow on very large projects (bundle time + sourcemaps). From our tests Webpack 5 (in development and production mode) seems to be a little faster and much much faster when it rebuilds via its watcher.

Webpack 5's watcher however doesn't provide enough granular control. From the looks of it you can't simply tell it, 'ignore this file change because something else is working, okay that is done now you can run'.

With the `watchOptions` you can provide a delay or ignore things entirely. we need more of a deferred execution.
If we were to just use Webpack's `watch` we would either have some unnecessary builds trying to run while things elsewhere are changing, or a consistent slow delay before it kick-starts.

Additionally it would not be super optimal to run multiple file watchers (running Webpack's on top of the one we are using already which covers what we need to care about).
author: Exigerr

body:
Not had much luck with the `cache` / `getCache` API.

I have found a potential way to deal with this but unsure if there is a better solution.
- Create a custom plugin which makes use of `stillValidModule` hook (`compilation.hooks.stillValidModule`).
- Update the plugin reference with a list of files which it should care about
- When the next build is manually triggered (with memory cache), `stillValidModule` will be called for the cached files.
- If the module provided to the `stillValidModule` hook matches one of the files, then call `compilation.rebuildModule` with it.

From quick tests that seems to correctly get Webpack to grab the new file and rebuild the chunks as needed.

Not sure if there is a better hook or not.
`stillValidModule` is listed in the types and noted on release notes, however there seems to be little to no information about it or what it should be used for.
Source code only shows that it is called after `needBuild` and it says it doesn't need to build the module again.
author: Exigerr

body:
Apologies for the slow reply.
I had tried the `NormaModule` `needBuild` hook previously and noticed it wasn't being called. After your comment, I went back and investigated further....silly me found that we still had the `speed-measure-webpack-v5-plugin` in use. That unfortunately breaks reference equality on the `compilation` object (hooks would never be called on the right thing due how `getCompilationHooks` works).

After removing the `speed-measure-webpack-v5-plugin` then retesting the `needBuild` hook again, it worked perfectly and I can easily make use of it for our use case!
Thank you very much for the assistance.

It looks like my problem is with concatenated modules.  If a module has been concatenated, then `compilation.moduleGraph.getOutgoingConnections` for that module is missing connections from the dynamic imports.  If I do `getOutgoingConnections` for the ConcatenatedModule itself, the dynamic import connections are there, but they are mixed in with all of the outgoing connections for any of the modules included in the concatenation.  I can't tell which are the connections for the specific module.

The incoming connections don't seem to have this problem.  If I get `compilation.moduleGraph.getIncomingConnections` for a module, it contains all incoming connections.

At the moment, to get the correct outgoing connection info, I need to iterate all of the incoming connection for all modules and invert those to figure out the corresponding outgoing connections.

This seems wrong, but maybe there's a reason for it.  Is it a bug or intended behavior?

body:
I did some more digging and I think I understand the difference now.

Webpack itself is not rewriting my application code, but webpack 4 and webpack 5 produce a different browser runtime environment.

In webpack 4, the bundle includes the old automatic Node/browser polyfills, including `timers-browserify` / `setimmediate`. Because of that, React’s scheduler sees `setImmediate` and uses that scheduling path.

In webpack 5, those automatic Node polyfills were removed, so React’s scheduler no longer sees `setImmediate` and falls back to `MessageChannel`.

That matters specifically in Safari/WebKit because popup/user-activation behavior differs depending on which async mechanism is used. WebKit has an open issue where user gesture context is propagated through `window.postMessage`, but not through `MessageChannel`: https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=186593

So the likely chain is:

- webpack 4 -> `setImmediate` polyfill -> internally uses `window.postMessage` -> Safari preserves enough user activation -> `window.open()` works
- webpack 5 -> no `setImmediate` polyfill -> React scheduler uses `MessageChannel` -> Safari loses user activation -> popup is blocked

This also explains why it only shows up on Safari/iOS and why Chrome/Firefox desktop behave differently.

So I agree this is probably not a webpack code transformation bug. It looks like a migration side effect from webpack 5 removing automatic Node polyfills, combined with WebKit’s user-activation behavior around `MessageChannel`.

Relevant webpack change: https://webpack.js.org/blog/2020-10-10-webpack-5-release/#automatic-nodejs-polyfills-removed

body:
The 'initial', 'async', and 'all' values for the splitChunks.chunks option in webpack determine which chunks are targeted for optimization:

- 'initial': This value targets the entry chunk(s) of the application.
- 'async': It targets the dynamically imported (asynchronous) chunks.
- 'all': It includes all types of chunks, both initial and async.
Using a function provides even more fine-grained control over which chunks are selected for optimization. The function receives the chunk as an argument and should return a truthy value to indicate that the chunk should be optimized.

By choosing one of these values or defining a custom function, you can customize how webpack's SplitChunksPlugin optimizes and splits chunks in your application.

P.S. it's answered by AI chat bot, It's convenient to solve usage problems with ChatGPT or other AI bot.

body:
We recommend using `all` by default, and you should stick to this strategy if you don't know. Sometimes it is useful for application performance to make more complex and detailed settings, you can want to reduce size of initial chunks (and load lazy async chunks), also you can want to merge all node_modules into one chunk and preload it before, you can want to merge all CSS chunks into one. 

It’s difficult to say which strategy will be best for you, because without knowing the structure of the application and the requirements for it, choosing the right settings is quite difficult. We have https://github.com/webpack-contrib/webpack-bundle-analyzer to help with it, there are also other tools which can show you and give advices, for example - https://github.com/statoscope/statoscope.

body:
Because you can have two different types of errors:
- Cirical errors, when compilation failed and webpack can't generate bundle, for example - you don't have access to write something in the `dist` output directory  
- Basic errors in modules/plugins/loaders, i.e. compilation was finished but with some errors, they are not critical and webpack generates output and we can output details of errors, for example - you are importing a non-installed package or some postcss plugin can do something but still render a CSS file

body:
Please use `"build": "webpack",` instead `"build": "webpack .",`, there is no `.`

Also please use `filename: '[name].bundle.js',`, because you have multiple chunk and can't use the same name

And please use:
```
new HtmlWebpackPlugin({
      template: path.join(__dirname, 'public', 'index1.html'),
      filename: "index1.html",
      chunks: ['chunkone']
    }),
     new HtmlWebpackPlugin({
      template: path.join(__dirname, 'public', 'index2.html'),
      filename: "index2.html",
      chunks: ['chunktwo']
    })
```

By default HtmlWebpackPlugin generates `index.html`

body:
> but when namedExport: true

Because it is a named export, so we don't use `default`, you can use:

```js
import { someClass } from './test.css'
```

> Why are those classNames not directly accessible from the default export in both cases? 

When you have `namedExport: false`, we use the `default` export for CSS code (i.e. CSS code itself and `@import`s), that's is why we need to use `locals` to export classes, we just can't export classes under the `default` export, but when you have `namedExport: true` we use named export to export classes and that is why you can write `import * as styles from './test.css'` or `import { someClass } from './test.css'`

body:
I wanted to share an update on the Webpack 5 caching issue we've been experiencing. After thorough investigation, we discovered that our custom Proxy cache object is being overwritten. Specifically, the `removeOperations` function in Webpack is executed recursively on resolve options, converting our Proxy cache into a plain empty object. This occurs just before the Webpack options are handed off to `enhanced-resolve`.

In `enhanced-resolve`, we should be able to pass a boolean or an object to `unsafeCache`. If an object is passed, that object will then be used as the cache itself. This functionality is crucial for maintaining custom caching logic in complex projects.

As such, I have [opened an issue](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/18129) with some more details. Looking forward to discussing how we can best solve this, if you are open to changing this behavior.

abrahamcuenca

body:
@abrahamcuenca Could you please try this first: remove your `exclude`-rule for `babel-loader` first and see whether the error would disappear:

```diff
- exclude: /node_modules|\.test\.jsx?$|\.snap$/,
```

I suspect that those .jsx tags might not be processed due to this exclusion rule. IIRC, a testing framework might expect to see the older syntax(i.e. not ES6, or it's called CJS, anyway) so you might have to transpile some of your testing files if you're writing in ES6+. (the arrow function in your case implies this.)
author: nyngwang

body: Thank you, @nyngwang. This resolved the issue with importing test files. I appreciate your time and assistance!
author: abrahamcuenca

body: @abrahamcuenca If you need to exclude files in `/node_modules` and/or `.snap`, you still need to add them back to make Babel transpile your project correctly, e.g. in your case, you probably still want to avoid transpiling third-party packages and snapshots with Babel. The point to keep in mind is that a testing framework usually written at the time ESM was not popular, so you have to transpile your modern EJS project into CJS by Babel before it can be read by the testing framework. By the way, you're welcome.
author: nyngwang
